When you talk, you prefer to speak with facts which you have double-checked yourself or cite sources that provide evidence, like quote actual code or give references to documents or pages found on the internet. You are polite and patient, and prefer to assume good intent, trying your best to be helpful. If you are unsure or have assumptions, you prefer to test them yourself or ask questions to clarify requirements.
General guidelines.
   - When you execute commands, always save their logs to files for easier reading if the output becomes large.
   - When running commands, do not set a timeout yourself — let them run as long as needed (default timeout - 2 minutes is more than enough), and once they finish, review the logs in the file.
   - When running sudo commands (especially package installations like apt-get, yum, npm install, etc.), always run them in the background to avoid timeout issues and permission errors when the process needs to be killed. Use the run_in_background parameter or append & to the command.
   - When CI is failing or user reports failures, consider adding a detailed investigation protocol to your todo list with these steps:
      Step 1: List recent runs with timestamps using: gh run list --repo konard/p-vs-np --branch issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf --limit 5 --json databaseId,conclusion,createdAt,headSha
      Step 2: Verify runs are after the latest commit by checking timestamps and SHA
      Step 3: For each non-passing run, download logs to preserve them: gh run view {run-id} --repo konard/p-vs-np --log > ci-logs/{workflow}-{run-id}.log
      Step 4: Read each downloaded log file using Read tool to understand the actual failures
      Step 5: Report findings with specific errors and line numbers from logs
      This detailed investigation is especially helpful when user mentions CI failures, asks to investigate logs, you see non-passing status, or when finalizing a PR.
      Note: If user says \"failing\" but tools show \"passing\", this might indicate stale data - consider downloading fresh logs and checking timestamps to resolve the discrepancy.
   - When a code or log file has more than 1500 lines, read it in chunks of 1500 lines.
   - When facing a complex problem, do as much tracing as possible and turn on all verbose modes.
   - When you create debug, test, or example/experiment scripts for fixing, always keep them in an ./examples and/or ./experiments folders so you can reuse them later.
   - When testing your assumptions, use the experiment scripts, and add it to ./experiments folder.
   - When your experiments can show real world use case of the software, add it to ./examples folder.
   - When you face something extremely hard, use divide and conquer — it always helps.

Initial research.
   - When you start, make sure you create detailed plan for yourself and follow your todo list step by step, make sure that as many points from these guidelines are added to your todo list to keep track of everything that can help you solve the issue with highest possible quality.
   - When user mentions CI failures or asks to investigate logs, consider adding these todos to track the investigation: (1) List recent CI runs with timestamps, (2) Download logs from failed runs to ci-logs/ directory, (3) Analyze error messages and identify root cause, (4) Implement fix, (5) Verify fix resolves the specific errors found in logs.
   - When you read issue, read all details and comments thoroughly.
   - When you see screenshots or images in issue descriptions, pull request descriptions, comments, or discussions, download the image to a local file first, then use Read tool to view and analyze it. IMPORTANT: Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify the file is a valid image (not HTML). Use a CLI tool like 'file' command to check the actual file format. Reading corrupted or non-image files (like GitHub's \"Not Found\" pages saved as .png) can cause \"Could not process image\" errors and will crash the AI solver process. If the file command shows \"HTML\", \"text\", or \"ASCII text\", the download FAILED — do NOT call Read on this file. Instead: (1) For images from GitHub issues/PRs (URLs containing \"github.com/user-attachments\"), these require authentication — retry with: curl -L -H \"Authorization: token \$(gh auth token)\" -o <filename> \"<url>\" (2) If retry still fails, skip the image and note it was unavailable.
   - When you need issue details, use gh issue view https://github.com/konard/p-vs-np/issues/453.
   - When you need related code, use gh search code --owner konard [keywords].
   - When you need repo context, read files in your working directory.
   - When you study related work, study the most recent related pull requests.
   - When issue is not defined enough, write a comment to ask clarifying questions.
   - When accessing GitHub Gists (especially private ones), use gh gist view command instead of direct URL fetching to ensure proper authentication.
   - When you are fixing a bug, please make sure you first find the actual root cause, do as many experiments as needed.
   - When you are fixing a bug and code does not have enough tracing/logs, add them and make sure they stay in the code, but are switched off by default.
   - When you need comments on a pull request, note that GitHub has THREE different comment types with different API endpoints:
      1. PR review comments (inline code comments): gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/pulls/515/comments --paginate
      2. PR conversation comments (general discussion): gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/issues/515/comments --paginate
      3. PR reviews (approve/request changes): gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/pulls/515/reviews --paginate
      IMPORTANT: The command \"gh pr view --json comments\" ONLY returns conversation comments and misses review comments!
   - When you need latest comments on issue, use gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/issues/453/comments --paginate.

Solution development and testing.
   - When issue is solvable, implement code with tests.
   - When implementing features, search for similar existing implementations in the codebase and use them as examples instead of implementing everything from scratch.
   - When coding, each atomic step that can be useful by itself should be commited to the pull request's branch, meaning if work will be interrupted by any reason parts of solution will still be kept intact and safe in pull request.
   - When you test:
      start from testing of small functions using separate scripts;
      write unit tests with mocks for easy and quick start.
   - When you test integrations, use existing framework.
   - When you test solution draft, include automated checks in pr.
   - When issue is unclear, write comment on issue asking questions.

   - When you need human help, use gh pr comment 515 --body \"your message\" to comment on existing PR.

   - When you code, follow contributing guidelines.
   - When you commit, write clear message.
   - When you need examples of style, use gh pr list --repo konard/p-vs-np --state merged --search [keywords].
   - When you open pr, describe solution draft and include tests.
   - When there is a package with version and GitHub Actions workflows for automatic release, update the version (or other necessary release trigger) in your pull request to prepare for next release.
   - When you update existing pr 515, use gh pr edit to modify title and description.
   - When you are about to commit or push code, ALWAYS run local CI checks first if they are available in contributing guidelines (like ruff check, mypy, eslint, etc.) to catch errors before pushing.
   - When you finalize the pull request:
      follow style from merged prs for code, title, and description,
      make sure no uncommitted changes corresponding to the original requirements are left behind,
      make sure the default branch is merged to the pull request's branch,
      make sure all CI checks passing if they exist before you finish,
      check for latest comments on the issue and pull request to ensure no recent feedback was missed,
      double-check that all changes in the pull request answer to original requirements of the issue,
      make sure no new new bugs are introduced in pull request by carefully reading gh pr diff,
      make sure no previously existing features were removed without an explicit request from users via the issue description, issue comments, and/or pull request comments.
   - When you finish implementation, use gh pr ready 515.

Workflow and collaboration.
   - When you check branch, verify with git branch --show-current.
   - When you push, push only to branch issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf.

Self review.
   - When you check your solution draft, run all tests locally.
   - When you check your solution draft, verify git status shows a clean working tree with no uncommitted changes.
   - When you compare with repo style, use gh pr diff [number].
   - When you finalize, confirm code, tests, and description are consistent.

GitHub CLI command patterns.
   - IMPORTANT: Always use --paginate flag when fetching lists from GitHub API to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).
   - When listing PR review comments (inline code comments), use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
   - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/\${owner}/\${repo}/pulls/\${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.

Visual UI work and screenshots.
   - When you work on visual UI changes (frontend, CSS, HTML, design), include a render or screenshot of the final result in the pull request description.
   - When you need to show visual results, take a screenshot and save it to the repository (e.g., in a docs/screenshots/ or assets/ folder).
   - When you save screenshots to the repository, use permanent links in the pull request description markdown (e.g., https://github.com/konard/p-vs-np/blob/issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf/docs/screenshots/result.png?raw=true).
   - When uploading images, commit them to the branch first, then reference them using the GitHub blob URL format with ?raw=true suffix (works for both public and private repositories).
   - When the visual result is important for review, mention it explicitly in the pull request description with the embedded image." | jq -c .)

IMPORTANT: Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify the file is a valid image (not HTML). Use a CLI tool like 'file' command to check the actual file format. Reading corrupted or non-image files (like GitHub's "Not Found" pages saved as .png) can cause "Could not process image" errors and will crash the AI solver process. If the file command shows "HTML", "text", or "ASCII text", the download FAILED — do NOT call Read on this file. Instead: (1) For images from GitHub issues/PRs (URLs containing "github.com/user-attachments"), these require authentication — retry with: curl -L -H "Authorization: token $(gh auth token)" -o <filename> "<url>" (2) If retry still fails, skip the image and note it was unavailable.
   - When you need issue details, use gh issue view https://github.com/konard/p-vs-np/issues/453.
   - When you need related code, use gh search code --owner konard [keywords].
   - When you need repo context, read files in your working directory.
   - When you study related work, study the most recent related pull requests.
   - When issue is not defined enough, write a comment to ask clarifying questions.
   - When accessing GitHub Gists (especially private ones), use gh gist view command instead of direct URL fetching to ensure proper authentication.
   - When you are fixing a bug, please make sure you first find the actual root cause, do as many experiments as needed.
   - When you are fixing a bug and code does not have enough tracing/logs, add them and make sure they stay in the code, but are switched off by default.
   - When you need comments on a pull request, note that GitHub has THREE different comment types with different API endpoints:
      1. PR review comments (inline code comments): gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/pulls/515/comments --paginate
      2. PR conversation comments (general discussion): gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/issues/515/comments --paginate
      3. PR reviews (approve/request changes): gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/pulls/515/reviews --paginate
      IMPORTANT: The command "gh pr view --json comments" ONLY returns conversation comments and misses review comments!
   - When you need latest comments on issue, use gh api repos/konard/p-vs-np/issues/453/comments --paginate.

Solution development and testing.
   - When issue is solvable, implement code with tests.
   - When implementing features, search for similar existing implementations in the codebase and use them as examples instead of implementing everything from scratch.
   - When coding, each atomic step that can be useful by itself should be commited to the pull request's branch, meaning if work will be interrupted by any reason parts of solution will still be kept intact and safe in pull request.
   - When you test:
      start from testing of small functions using separate scripts;
      write unit tests with mocks for easy and quick start.
   - When you test integrations, use existing framework.
   - When you test solution draft, include automated checks in pr.
   - When issue is unclear, write comment on issue asking questions.
   - When you encounter any problems that you unable to solve yourself (any human feedback or help), write a comment to the pull request asking for help.
   - When you need human help, use gh pr comment 515 --body "your message" to comment on existing PR.

Preparing pull request.
   - When you code, follow contributing guidelines.
   - When you commit, write clear message.
   - When you need examples of style, use gh pr list --repo konard/p-vs-np --state merged --search [keywords].
   - When you open pr, describe solution draft and include tests.
   - When there is a package with version and GitHub Actions workflows for automatic release, update the version (or other necessary release trigger) in your pull request to prepare for next release.
   - When you update existing pr 515, use gh pr edit to modify title and description.
   - When you are about to commit or push code, ALWAYS run local CI checks first if they are available in contributing guidelines (like ruff check, mypy, eslint, etc.) to catch errors before pushing.
   - When you finalize the pull request:
      follow style from merged prs for code, title, and description,
      make sure no uncommitted changes corresponding to the original requirements are left behind,
      make sure the default branch is merged to the pull request's branch,
      make sure all CI checks passing if they exist before you finish,
      check for latest comments on the issue and pull request to ensure no recent feedback was missed,
      double-check that all changes in the pull request answer to original requirements of the issue,
      make sure no new new bugs are introduced in pull request by carefully reading gh pr diff,
      make sure no previously existing features were removed without an explicit request from users via the issue description, issue comments, and/or pull request comments.
   - When you finish implementation, use gh pr ready 515.

Workflow and collaboration.
   - When you check branch, verify with git branch --show-current.
   - When you push, push only to branch issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf.
   - When you finish, create a pull request from branch issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf. (Note: PR 515 already exists, update it instead)
   - When you organize workflow, use pull requests instead of direct merges to default branch (main or master).
   - When you manage commits, preserve commit history for later analysis.
   - When you contribute, keep repository history forward-moving with regular commits, pushes, and reverts if needed.
   - When you face conflict that you cannot resolve yourself, ask for help.
   - When you collaborate, respect branch protections by working only on issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf.
   - When you mention result, include pull request url or comment url.
   - When you need to create pr, remember pr 515 already exists for this branch.

Self review.
   - When you check your solution draft, run all tests locally.
   - When you check your solution draft, verify git status shows a clean working tree with no uncommitted changes.
   - When you compare with repo style, use gh pr diff [number].
   - When you finalize, confirm code, tests, and description are consistent.

GitHub CLI command patterns.
   - IMPORTANT: Always use --paginate flag when fetching lists from GitHub API to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).
   - When listing PR review comments (inline code comments), use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
   - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/${owner}/${repo}/pulls/${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.

Visual UI work and screenshots.
   - When you work on visual UI changes (frontend, CSS, HTML, design), include a render or screenshot of the final result in the pull request description.
   - When you need to show visual results, take a screenshot and save it to the repository (e.g., in a docs/screenshots/ or assets/ folder).
   - When you save screenshots to the repository, use permanent links in the pull request description markdown (e.g., https://github.com/konard/p-vs-np/blob/issue-453-bbc740e7f8bf/docs/screenshots/result.png?raw=true).
   - When uploading images, commit them to the branch first, then reference them using the GitHub blob URL format with ?raw=true suffix (works for both public and private repositories).
   - When the visual result is important for review, mention it explicitly in the pull request description with the embedded image.

  "type": "user",
  "message": {
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
      {
        "tool_use_id": "toolu_0173s4e9Abj6vUBMKNqrqCix",
        "type": "tool_result",
        "content": "     1→# Sten-Ake Tarnlund (2008) - P≠NP via First-Order Theory and Universal Turing Machines\n     2→\n     3→**Attempt ID**: 48 (from Woeginger's list)\n     4→**Author**: Sten-Ake Tarnlund\n     5→**Year**: 2008 (revised 2009, updated through 2017)\n     6→**Claim**: P ≠ NP\n     7→**Status**: Refuted/Unaccepted\n     8→\n     9→## Summary\n    10→\n    11→In October 2008, Sten-Ake Tarnlund proposed a proof that P ≠ NP using a first-order logical theory of computing. The approach attempted to prove within a formal system that SAT (the Boolean satisfiability problem) cannot be solved in polynomial time, thereby establishing P ≠ NP. The proof was based on a single finite axiom characterizing a universal Turing machine and claimed to show that \"SAT is not in P\" is provable in a simply consistent extension of a first-order theory of computing.\n    12→\n    13→## Main Argument\n    14→\n    15→### The Approach\n    16→\n    17→1. **First-Order Theory Foundation**: Tarnlund developed a first-order theory B of computing with axioms characterizing a universal Turing machine\n    18→2. **Formal Extension**: He created a simply consistent extension B' of this theory\n    19→3. **Provability Claim**: Within this formal system, he claimed to prove that \"SAT is not in P\"\n    20→4. **Meta-Theoretical Conclusion**: From this provability result, he argued that P ≠ NP follows as a mathematical truth\n    21→\n    22→### Key Technical Claims\n    23→\n    24→The crucial elements were:\n    25→- A **single finite axiom** sufficient to characterize universal Turing machines\n    26→- A **simply consistent extension** of the base theory that can prove computational complexity bounds\n    27→- The ability to **formalize and prove** statements about polynomial-time computability within this system\n    28→- A **meta-theoretical bridge** from provability within the formal system to mathematical truth\n    29→\n    30→### Later Refinements\n    31→\n    32→Tarnlund revised the proof multiple times:\n    33→- **2009**: Reorganized into lemma, corollary, and proof sections\n    34→- **2013**: Announced conversion to formal proofs suitable for Hilbert's proof theory and automated theorem provers\n    35→- **2017**: Ninth edition with further simplifications\n    36→\n    37→## The Error\n    38→\n    39→### Fundamental Flaw: Confusion Between Provability and Truth\n    40→\n    41→**The Primary Error**: The proof conflates **provability within a formal system** with **mathematical truth about computational complexity**.\n    42→\n    43→**Why This Matters**:\n    44→- Just because you can prove \"SAT is not in P\" in a particular formal system doesn't mean this statement is actually true\n    45→- The formal system itself might be:\n    46→  1. Inconsistent (can prove both P=NP and P≠NP)\n    47→  2. Unsound (proves false statements about computation)\n    48→  3. Too weak to capture actual computational complexity\n    49→  4. Based on assumptions that don't correspond to real computation\n    50→\n    51→### Specific Issues Identified\n    52→\n    53→#### 1. Unclear Formal System\n    54→\n    55→**Critique by Henning Makholm (2008)**:\n    56→- The paper is \"pithy to the point of sloppiness\"\n    57→- The formal system and its axioms are not clearly specified\n    58→- The relationship between the formal theory and actual Turing machine computation is not rigorously established\n    59→\n    60→#### 2. SAT Redefinition Concerns\n    61→\n    62→Initial critiques suggested:\n    63→- The paper may be working with a non-standard definition of SAT\n    64→- The problem being solved in the formal system (PFm) may not be the standard Boolean SAT problem\n    65→- This would make the result irrelevant to the actual P vs NP question\n    66→\n    67→While purely propositional formalizations of problems like the pigeonhole principle exist, the paper's presentation made it difficult to verify what was actually being formalized.\n    68→\n    69→#### 3. The Soundness Gap\n    70→\n    71→**Critical Issue**: Even if the formal system proves \"SAT is not in P,\" this only matters if:\n    72→- The formal system correctly models actual computation\n    73→- The axioms are sound (represent true facts about Turing machines)\n    74→- The inference rules preserve truth about computational complexity\n    75→\n    76→None of these were rigorously established in the paper.\n    77→\n    78→#### 4. Meta-Theoretical Leap\n    79→\n    80→The argument requires:\n    81→1. Proving a statement within a formal system\n    82→2. Concluding that the statement is true in reality\n    83→\n    84→This leap requires:\n    85→- **Soundness**: The formal system only proves true statements\n    86→- **Adequacy**: The formal system can express computational complexity correctly\n    87→- **Consistency**: The formal system doesn't prove contradictions\n    88→\n    89→These properties were not proven for Tarnlund's system.\n    90→\n    91→## Why This Approach Cannot Work (General Principle)\n    92→\n    93→### Gödel's Incompleteness and Complexity\n    94→\n    95→There's a fundamental barrier to this approach:\n    96→\n    97→1. **Self-Reference Problem**: Any formal system strong enough to reason about Turing machines can encode questions about its own provability\n    98→2. **Incompleteness**: By Gödel's theorems, no consistent formal system can prove all true statements about computation\n    99→3. **Complexity Independence**: If P ≠ NP is true, it's likely true for \"simple\" computational reasons that don't require sophisticated formal systems to establish\n   100→\n   101→### The Oracle Problem\n   102→\n   103→Even if we could formalize computational complexity:\n   104→- We would need to prove that no polynomial-time algorithm for SAT exists\n   105→- This requires proving a **universal negative** (\"for all algorithms...\")\n   106→- Formal systems struggle with such universal statements about infinite domains\n   107→- We would need the formal system to somehow \"know\" about all possible algorithms\n   108→\n   109→## Broader Context\n   110→\n   111→### Why This Approach Is Tempting\n   112→\n   113→The approach appears promising because:\n   114→- Formal logic has been successful in other areas of mathematics\n   115→- Hilbert's program sought to formalize all of mathematics\n   116→- Modern automated theorem provers have proven complex results\n   117→- P vs NP is a mathematical question, so it should be formalizable\n   118→\n   119→### The Critical Distinction: Syntax vs Semantics\n   120→\n   121→- **Syntax**: What can be proven in a formal system (syntactic manipulation of symbols)\n   122→- **Semantics**: What is actually true about computation (real algorithms running on real inputs)\n   123→- **The Gap**: Bridging syntax and semantics requires soundness proofs, which are difficult and domain-specific\n   124→\n   125→Tarnlund's error was assuming this gap could be easily bridged without rigorous proof.\n   126→\n   127→## Reception and Legacy\n   128→\n   129→The claim has been:\n   130→- **Critiqued**: By Henning Makholm and others in 2008\n   131→- **Revised**: Multiple times by Tarnlund (2009, 2013, 2017)\n   132→- **Unaccepted**: The complexity theory community does not accept this as a valid proof\n   133→- **Unverified**: No independent verification or acceptance in peer-reviewed venues\n   134→\n   135→The fact that the proof has been revised many times without gaining acceptance suggests fundamental issues rather than minor technical errors.\n   136→\n   137→## Formalization Goals\n   138→\n   139→In this directory, we formalize:\n   140→\n   141→1. **The Basic Claim**: What it means to prove computational complexity results in a formal system\n   142→2. **The Soundness Requirement**: Why the formal system must correctly model reality\n   143→3. **The Gap**: Where the proof fails (soundness is not established)\n   144→4. **Key Lessons**: Why mixing provability and truth is dangerous\n   145→\n   146→The formalization demonstrates that:\n   147→- The approach is conceptually clear but technically flawed\n   148→- The critical step (proving soundness) is non-trivial and not accomplished\n   149→- Without soundness, provability in a formal system tells us nothing about reality\n   150→\n   151→## References\n   152→\n   153→### Primary Sources\n   154→\n   155→- **Original Paper**: Tarnlund, S.-A. (2008). \"P is not equal to NP\"\n   156→  - arXiv:0810.5056v1 [cs.CC] (28 Oct 2008)\n   157→  - Available at: https://arxiv.org/abs/0810.5056\n   158→- **Revised Version**: Tarnlund, S.-A. (2009). \"P is not equal to NP\"\n   159→  - arXiv:0810.5056v2 [cs.CC] (13 Jul 2009)\n   160→- **Later Version**: Tarnlund, S.-A. (2017). \"P is not equal to NP\" (Ninth edition)\n   161→  - DiVA Portal: diva2:1142225\n   162→\n   163→### Critiques\n   164→\n   165→- **Makholm Blog Post**: Makholm, H. (2008). \"Does P equal NP? This is not an answer\"\n   166→  - What the Hedgehog Sang blog\n   167→  - Available at: http://blog.henning.makholm.net/2008/11/does-p-equal-np-this-is-not-answer.html\n   168→- **Schneier Commentary**: Schneier, B. (2008). Noted that \"these sorts of papers make the rounds regularly\" and recommended skepticism\n   169→\n   170→### Context\n   171→\n   172→- **Woeginger's List**: Entry #48\n   173→  - https://wscor.win.tue.nl/woeginger/P-versus-NP.htm\n   174→- **arXiv Category**: Computer Science - Computational Complexity (cs.CC)\n   175→\n   176→## Key Lessons\n   177→\n   178→1. **Provability ≠ Truth**: Proving something in a formal system doesn't make it true in reality\n   179→2. **Soundness is Critical**: Formal systems must be proven sound for their domain\n   180→3. **Gödel's Limitations**: Formal systems have inherent limitations in what they can prove\n   181→4. **Clarity Matters**: Vague formal systems make verification impossible\n   182→5. **Peer Review**: Unreviewed claims, especially revised many times, require skepticism\n   183→6. **Meta-Theory is Hard**: Reasoning about what formal systems can prove is subtle\n   184→\n   185→## See Also\n   186→\n   187→- [P ≠ NP Framework](../../p_neq_np/) - General framework for evaluating P ≠ NP claims\n   188→- [Proof Experiments](../../experiments/) - Other experimental approaches to P vs NP\n   189→- [Repository README](../../../README.md) - Overview of the P vs NP problem\n   190→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

  "type": "user",
  "message": {
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
      {
        "tool_use_id": "toolu_01Tt7fEY84PnuSsreC3jUQjt",
        "type": "tool_result",
        "content": "     1→# Original Paper: P is not equal to NP\n     2→\n     3→**Author:** Sten-Ake Tarnlund\n     4→**Year:** 2008 (arXiv:0810.5056v1, submitted October 28, 2008)\n     5→**arXiv ID:** [arXiv:0810.5056](https://arxiv.org/abs/0810.5056)\n     6→**Subject Classification:** Computational Complexity (cs.CC); Logic in Computer Science (cs.LO)\n     7→**Woeginger's List:** Entry #48\n     8→\n     9→---\n    10→\n    11→## Abstract\n    12→\n    13→SAT ∉ P is true, and provable in a simply consistent extension B' of a first order theory **B** of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine. Therefore P ≠ NP is true, and provable in a simply consistent extension B'' of **B**.\n    14→\n    15→---\n    16→\n    17→## 1. Introduction\n    18→\n    19→SAT ∉ P is true, theorem 1, and provable, corollary 9, in a simply consistent extension B' of a first order theory **B** of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine. Therefore, by the Cook-Levin theorem (SAT ∈ P ≡ P = NP, Cook 1971, Levin 1973), P ≠ NP is true, theorem 2, and provable, corollary 10, in a simply consistent extension B'' of theory **B**.\n    20→\n    21→SAT is the set of satisfiable propositional formulas. P and NP are sets of classes of problems of polynomial time complexity for deterministic and nondeterministic Turing machines respectively.\n    22→\n    23→The chief idea of the proof of theorem 1 is a relationship between computing time and proof complexity, in fact, the principal lemma 3, which essentially is developed from axiom B. First, there is a relationship between a formal proof in theory **B**, and a formula in propositional logic. However, lemma 3 gives more i.e., the number of tapehead moves of a deterministic Turing machine computing unsatisfiability of ¬F expressed as a simple formula in propositional logic, from which the tautology F is deducible. More precisely: if there is a deterministic Turing machine i ∈ U that computes unsatisfiability of ¬F in computing time n polynomial in the size of F then Y(i, F, n), for a sufficiently large tautology F.\n    24→\n    25→More to the point, by definition 20, Y(i, F, n) is equivalent to:\n    26→\n    27→```\n    28→(Q₀ ⊃ F) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁)  some single letters Q₀,...,Qₙ   (1)\n    29→```\n    30→\n    31→In propositional logic there is a formal deduction of the tautology F in polynomial time in the size of F if Y(i, F, n) is true. There is now an indirect proof of theorem 1.\n    32→\n    33→Assume that\n    34→\n    35→```\n    36→SAT ∈ P.                                                                    (2)\n    37→```\n    38→\n    39→By lemma 3, and a sufficiently large pigeonhole formula PFₘ,\n    40→\n    41→```\n    42→Y(i, PFₘ, n) and n ≤ c·|PFₘ|^q  some c q ∈ ℕ  i ∈ U  a sufficiently large m.  (3)\n    43→```\n    44→\n    45→Thus, by (1),\n    46→\n    47→```\n    48→(Q₀ ⊃ PFₘ) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁)  some single letters Q₀,...,Qₙ.    (4)\n    49→```\n    50→\n    51→By Robinson's resolution, and (3) - (4), no more than c·|PFₘ|^q resolvents are constructed in the deduction of PFₘ from (4), and the size of the formal deduction is polynomial in the size of PFₘ. Therefore\n    52→\n    53→```\n    54→⊢_R PFₘ, with a polynomial size of the deduction in the size of PFₘ.         (5)\n    55→```\n    56→\n    57→Hence, a contradiction between (5), Haken's theorem (every resolution proof of PFₙ contains at least cⁿ different clauses for c > 1 some c ∈ ℝ any sufficiently large n ∈ ℕ), and the fact that theory **B** is simply consistent i.e., has no contradiction, for the subset of the deterministic Turing machines that compute satisfiability or unsatisfiability of propositional formulas, corollary 2.\n    58→\n    59→Thus\n    60→\n    61→```\n    62→SAT ∉ P.                                                                    (6)\n    63→```\n    64→\n    65→It remains to lay down the first order theory **B** of computing, with axiom B, and to prove the principal lemma 3. Then a proof of theorem 1 follows in the proof (meta) theory of **B**, as outlined in (2) - (6).\n    66→\n    67→---\n    68→\n    69→## 2. A theory of computing\n    70→\n    71→Before applying the axiomatic method to computing complexity, a first order theory **B** of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine, is presented.\n    72→\n    73→Syntactically, there are two predicate symbols of **B** written T(i, a, u), and U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u). In addition, one function symbol . in infix notation x.y.\n    74→\n    75→A Turing machine has a finite supply of arbitrary constant symbols, for example, the alphabetic symbols A, a, B, b, ..., the natural numbers, and the symbols of propositional logic. For convenience, there is at least a subset\n    76→\n    77→```\n    78→{∅, 0, 1, ⊔} ⊆ K,                                                          (7)\n    79→```\n    80→\n    81→where K is a finite set of constant symbols, and ⊔ a blank symbol.\n    82→\n    83→There are six sets in **B**.\n    84→\n    85→A finite set\n    86→\n    87→```\n    88→Q ⊂ ℕ of states,                                                            (8)\n    89→```\n    90→\n    91→where 0 is the halt state and 1 the start state.\n    92→\n    93→A finite set S of symbols,\n    94→\n    95→```\n    96→S for { u : u ∈ K ∨ (u = r.∅ ∨ u = ∅.r) ∧ r ∈ K}.                         (9)\n    97→```\n    98→\n    99→A set D of moves of the tapehead of a Turing machine,\n   100→\n   101→```\n   102→D for {0, 1},                                                               (10)\n   103→```\n   104→\n   105→where 0 is a move to the left and 1 a move to the right.\n   106→\n   107→There is a finite arbitrary large two-way tape, with a left and right tape having an element between them at the tapehead. Initially, the two-way tape has an empty left tape, the input on the right tape, and the element between them has the symbol ∅. When a computation starts the tapehead reads the symbol ∅.\n   108→\n   109→There are two sets L and L' of lists, representing the right tape, and left tape respectively:\n   110→\n   111→```\n   112→L(∅) ∧ ∀ (s ∈ S ∧ L(z) ⊃ L(s.z)), and                                     (11)\n   113→L'(∅) ∧ ∀ (s ∈ S ∧ L'(z) ⊃ L'(z.s))                                       (12)\n   114→```\n   115→\n   116→Then,\n   117→\n   118→```\n   119→L for {u : L(u)}.                                                           (13)\n   120→L' for {u : L'(u)}.                                                         (14)\n   121→```\n   122→\n   123→A set M ⊂ L of codes of Turing machines:\n   124→\n   125→```\n   126→M(∅) ∧ ∀ (p q ∈ Q ∧ r s ∈ S ∧ d ∈ D ∧ M(z) ⊃ M(p.s.q.r.d.z)).           (15)\n   127→```\n   128→\n   129→Then the set of codes of Turing machines,\n   130→\n   131→```\n   132→M for {u : M(u)}.                                                           (16)\n   133→```\n   134→\n   135→**Definition 1** The set PROP of atomic propositional formulas of theory **B** has the elements U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u), and T(i, a, u) i j ∈ M a z ∈ L x u ∈ L' q ∈ Q s ∈ S.\n   136→\n   137→**Definition 2** The set ATOM of atomic formulas of theory **B** has as members the elements in PROP, and an atomic formula constructed by substituting a variable for a term in an atomic propositional formula in PROP.\n   138→\n   139→**Definition 3** The set of formulas of theory **B** has as members the elements in ATOM, and an element constructed by the formation rules of predicate calculus from elements in ATOM.\n   140→\n   141→Semantically, the infix function symbol and the predicate symbols denote two functions and two relations.\n   142→\n   143→- T(i, a, u) shall mean Turing machine i with input a computes an output u and then halts for i ∈ M a ∈ L u ∈ L'.\n   144→- U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u) shall mean Turing machine i computes u and then halts, where x.s.z is the two-way tape of i, s is a symbol (at the tapehead), x is the left tape, z is the right tape, i is in state q, and has an auxiliary code j, for i j ∈ M s ∈ S z ∈ L q ∈ Q x u ∈ L'.\n   145→\n   146→### Axiom 1 (B)\n   147→\n   148→```\n   149→∀ T(i, a, u) ⊃ U(∅, ∅, a.∅, 1, i, i, u)   i ∈ M a ∈ L u ∈ L'.            (17)\n   150→∀ U(∅, ∅, a.∅, 1, i, i, u) ⊃ T(i, a, u)   a ∈ L i ∈ M u ∈ L'.            (18)\n   151→∀ U(x, s, z, 0, i, i, x)   x ∈ L' s ∈ S z ∈ L i ∈ M.                     (19)\n   152→∀ U(x, v, r.z, p, i, i, u) ⊃ U(x.v, s, z, q, q.s.p.r.0.j, i, u)          (20)\n   153→  x u ∈ L' v r s ∈ S z ∈ L p q ∈ Q i j ∈ M.\n   154→∀ U(x.r, v, z, p, i, i, u) ⊃ U(x, s, v.z, q, q.s.p.r.1.j, i, u)          (21)\n   155→  x u ∈ L' r v s ∈ S z ∈ L p q ∈ Q i j ∈ M.\n   156→∀ U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u) ⊃ U(x, s, z, q, q'.s'.p.r.d.j, i, u)            (22)\n   157→  x u ∈ L' s s' r ∈ S z ∈ L q q' p ∈ Q j i ∈ M d ∈ D.\n   158→```\n   159→\n   160→Sentences (17)-(18) give equivalence between T(i,a,u) and the concrete representation. Sentence (19) is the halt condition. Sentences (20)-(21) handle left/right moves. Sentence (22) handles quintuple searching.\n   161→\n   162→**Definition 4** D = {i : i has no quintuples q.s.p.r.d and q.s.p'.r'.d' and ¬(p = p' ∧ r = r' ∧ d = d') for i ∈ M}.\n   163→\n   164→**Lemma 1** ∃uT(i, a, u) ⊃ ⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u) any i ∈ M a ∈ L.\n   165→\n   166→**Definition 5** A Turing machine computation for a formal proof in theory **B**.\n   167→\n   168→---\n   169→\n   170→## 3. Computing time\n   171→\n   172→**Definition 6** H(⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u), n) for there is a formal proof of the sequent B → ∃uT(i, a, u) in G4 with n moves of the tapehead of a Turing machine i with an input a computing an output, any i ∈ M a ∈ L n ∈ ℕ.\n   173→\n   174→**Definition 7** |a| for the number of symbols of a ∈ L.\n   175→\n   176→**Definition 8** p(a) for c·|a|^q some c q ∈ ℕ any a ∈ L.\n   177→\n   178→**Definition 9** ⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u) in p(a) for H(⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u), n) ∧ n ≤ c·|a|^q any i ∈ M some c q ∈ ℕ any a ∈ L some n ∈ ℕ.\n   179→\n   180→**Definition 10** F for the set of formulas of propositional logic.\n   181→\n   182→**Definition 11** SAT for {F : F ∈ F ∧ ⊭ ¬F}.\n   183→\n   184→**Definition 12** TAUT for {F : F ∈ F ∧ ⊨ F}.\n   185→\n   186→**Definition 13** T(s, a, u) for a = F.∅ ∧ ((u = ∅.0 ∧ ⊭ ¬F) ∨ (u = ∅.1 ∧ ⊨ ¬F)) any a ∈ L F ∈ F u ∈ L.\n   187→\n   188→**Definition 14** U for { i : ∃u(T(i, a, u) ∧ T(s, a, u)) ∧ i ∈ D ∧ any a ∈ L }.\n   189→\n   190→**Corollary 1** T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) ≡ F any i ∈ U any F ∈ TAUT.\n   191→\n   192→**Corollary 2** ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, a, u) ∧ ¬∃u T(i, a, u) no i ∈ U a ∈ L.\n   193→\n   194→**Corollary 3** ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, a, u) ≡ ∃u T(i, a, u) any i ∈ U a ∈ L.\n   195→\n   196→**Definition 15** SAT ∈ P for ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, F.∅, u) in p(F) some i ∈ U any F ∈ F.\n   197→\n   198→**Corollary 4** ⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) ≡ ⊨ → F any i ∈ U F ∈ F.\n   199→\n   200→**Corollary 5** If SAT ∈ P then ⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F) some i ∈ U any F ∈ TAUT.\n   201→\n   202→---\n   203→\n   204→## 4. Computing time and proof complexity\n   205→\n   206→A relationship between computing time and proof complexity is established in the principal lemma 3, using lemma 2, and axiom B.\n   207→\n   208→**Definition 16** V(i, F, n) for\n   209→\n   210→```\n   211→(U(x₀, s₀, z₀, q₀, i, i, ∅.1) ⊃ T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1)) ∧                      (35)\n   212→U(∅.1, sₙ, zₙ, 0, i, i, ∅.1) ∧                                            (36)\n   213→⋀₁≤m≤n ((U(xₘ, sₘ, zₘ, qₘ, i, i, ∅.1) ⊃                                  (37)\n   214→  U(xₘ₋₁, sₘ₋₁, zₘ₋₁, qₘ₋₁, jₘ₋₁, i, ∅.1)) ∧\n   215→  (U(xₘ₋₁, sₘ₋₁, zₘ₋₁, qₘ₋₁, jₘ₋₁, i, ∅.1) ⊃\n   216→  U(xₘ₋₁, sₘ₋₁, zₘ₋₁, qₘ₋₁, i, i, ∅.1)))\n   217→any i ∈ U F ∈ TAUT n ∈ ℕ\n   218→```\n   219→\n   220→**Lemma 2** H(⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1), n) ⊃ V(i, F, n) any i ∈ U F ∈ any i ∈ U ... some single letters Rₘ₊₁ ∈ F.\n   225→\n   226→**Definition 19** W(i, F, n) for Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤m≤n (Rₘ ⊃ Qₘ₋₁) ∧ (Qₘ ⊃ Rₘ) ∧ (Q₀ ⊃ T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1)) any i ∈ U F ∈ TAUT n ∈ ℕ some single letters A first-order theory B with axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine\n    11→  2. A relationship between computing time and proof complexity (Lemma 3)\n    12→  3. Haken's theorem on resolution proof complexity of pigeonhole formulas\n    13→\n    14→  This formalization captures what Tarnlund CLAIMED, showing each step of the\n    15→  original paper as Lean definitions and axioms.\n    16→-/\n    17→\n    18→namespace TarnlundProof\n    19→\n    20→/-! ## Section 1: Introduction - Overview of the Argument\n    21→\n    22→  From the paper (Section 1):\n    23→  \"SAT ∉ P is true, theorem 1, and provable, corollary 9, in a simply consistent\n    24→  extension B' of a first order theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom\n    25→  B characterizing a universal Turing machine.\"\n    26→-/\n    27→\n    28→/-! ## Section 2: A Theory of Computing\n    29→\n    30→  From the paper (Section 2):\n    31→  \"Before applying the axiomatic method to computing complexity, a first order\n    32→  theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal\n    33→  Turing machine, is presented.\"\n    34→-/\n    35→\n    36→/-- Binary strings as inputs -/\n    37→def Language := String → Bool\n    38→\n    39→/-- Time complexity function -/\n    40→def TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n    41→\n    42→/-- Polynomial time (Definition 8: p(a) for c·|a|^q some c q ∈ ℕ) -/\n    43→def isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    44→  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n    45→\n    46→/-- Class P: polynomial-time decidable languages -/\n    47→structure ClassP where\n    48→  language : Language\n    49→  decider : String → Nat\n    50→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    51→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    52→\n    53→/-- Class NP: polynomial-time verifiable languages -/\n    54→structure ClassNP where\n    55→  language : Language\n    56→  verifier : String → String → Bool\n    57→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    58→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    59→\n    60→/-- SAT problem (axiomatically NP-complete) -/\n    61→axiom SAT : ClassNP\n    62→\n    63→/-- P ≠ NP statement -/\n    64→def PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    65→  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n    66→\n    67→/-! ## Section 2: Formal System Definitions\n    68→\n    69→  From the paper:\n    70→  \"Syntactically, there are two predicate symbols of B written T(i, a, u), and\n    71→  U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u).\"\n    72→-/\n    73→\n    74→/-- A formula in the formal system (Definition 3) -/\n    75→structure Formula where\n    76→  symbols : List String\n    77→\n    78→/-- A formal system with axioms and inference rules -/\n    79→structure FormalSystem where\n    80→  axioms : List Formula\n    81→  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n    82→\n    83→/-- Provability in a formal system -/\n    84→def Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n    85→  True  -- Abstract provability relation\n    86→\n    87→/-! ## Theory B and Axiom B (Axiom 1)\n    88→\n    89→  From the paper (Axiom 1):\n    90→  \"B for ∀ T(i, a, u) ⊃ U(∅, ∅, a.∅, 1, i, i, u) ...\"\n    91→  The axiom has six sentences (17)-(22) characterizing a universal Turing machine.\n    92→-/\n    93→\n    94→/-- Theory B (Tarnlund's base formal system) -/\n    95→def TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    96→  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n    97→\n    98→/-- Universal Turing Machine axiom (Axiom 1, sentences 17-22) -/\n    99→noncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n   100→\n   101→/-- Theory B' (Theory B + Universal TM axiom, simply consistent extension) -/\n   102→noncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n   103→  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n   104→    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n   105→\n   106→/-! ## Section 2: Consistency Claims\n   107→\n   108→  From the paper (Corollary 2):\n   109→  \"Theory B is simply consistent in U i.e., there is no contradiction\"\n   110→-/\n   111→\n   112→/-- Consistency of a formal system -/\n   113→def IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True  -- Abstract consistency\n   114→\n   115→/-- Simple consistency (Definition from Kleene 1967) -/\n   116→def IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n   117→\n   118→/-- Tarnlund claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2) -/\n   119→axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n   120→\n   121→/-! ## Section 2: Lemma 1\n   122→\n   123→  From the paper:\n   124→  \"Lemma 1: ∃uT(i, a, u) ⊃ ⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u) any i ∈ M a ∈ L.\"\n   125→  If a computation terminates, it is provable in theory B.\n   126→-/\n   127→\n   128→/-- Lemma 1: True computations are provable in theory B -/\n   129→axiom lemma1_computations_provable :\n   130→  ∀ (F : Formula), Provable TheoryBPrime F\n   131→\n   132→/-! ## Section 3: Computing Time (Definitions 6-15)\n   133→\n   134→  From the paper (Definition 15):\n   135→  \"SAT ∈ P for ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, F.∅, u) in p(F) some i ∈ U any F ∈ F.\"\n   136→-/\n   137→\n   138→/-- Formula representing \"SAT ∉ P\" -/\n   139→def SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n   140→  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n   141→\n   142→/-- What it means for a formula to represent a computational fact -/\n   143→def FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n   144→  True  -- Abstract meaning relation\n   145→\n   146→/-- Tarnlund claims to prove \"SAT ∉ P\" in TheoryB' (Theorem 1) -/\n   147→axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n   148→\n   149→/-- The formula is claimed to mean P ≠ NP -/\n   150→axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n   151→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n   152→\n   153→/-! ## Section 4: Computing Time and Proof Complexity\n   154→\n   155→  From the paper (Lemma 3):\n   156→  \"⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F) ⊃ Y(i, F, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|F|^q\"\n   157→\n   158→  This is the principal lemma establishing the relationship between\n   159→  computing time and proof complexity.\n   160→-/\n   161→\n   162→/-- Y(i, F, n) formula - Definition 20\n   163→    Y(i, F, n) for (Q₀ ⊃ F) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁)\n   164→    This represents the propositional formula that relates computing time\n   165→    to a chain of implications leading to the tautology F. -/\n   166→def Y_formula (_i : Nat) (_F : Formula) (_n : Nat) : Prop := True\n   167→\n   168→/-- Lemma 3 (Principal Lemma):\n   169→    If computing time is polynomial, then Y(i, F, n) holds with polynomial n.\n   170→    This is the key claim linking computing time to proof complexity. -/\n   171→axiom lemma3_computing_time_proof_complexity :\n   172→  ∀ (i : Nat) (F : Formula) (n : Nat),\n   173→    Y_formula i F n\n   174→\n   175→/-! ## Section 4.1: Proof Complexity and Haken's Theorem\n   176→\n   177→  From the paper:\n   178→  \"Every resolution proof of PFₙ contains at least cⁿ different clauses\n   179→  for c > 1 some c ∈ ℝ any sufficiently large n ∈ ℕ\" (Haken 1985)\n   180→-/\n   181→\n   182→/-- Resolution provability (Definition 22) -/\n   183→def ResolutionProvable (_F : Formula) (_size : Nat) : Prop := True\n   184→\n   185→/-- Haken's theorem: resolution proofs of pigeonhole formulas require\n   186→    exponentially many clauses -/\n   187→axiom hakens_theorem :\n   188→  ∀ (m : Nat), ∃ (minSize : Nat),\n   189→    (∀ (k : Nat), minSize > k * m ^ k) ∧\n   190→    ∀ (size : Nat), size < minSize → ¬ ResolutionProvable\n   191→      { symbols := [\"PF\", toString m] } size\n   192→\n   193→/-! ## Main Results: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2\n   194→\n   195→  From the paper (Theorem 1 proof, steps 46-53):\n   196→  ⋆ SAT ∈ P (assumption for contradiction)\n   197→  ⋆ ⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F)  (Corollary 5)\n   198→  ⋆ Y(i, PFₘ, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|PFₘ|^q  (Lemma 3)\n   199→  ⋆ ⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ)  (from Y formula)\n   200→  ⋆ ¬(⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ))  (Haken's theorem - CONTRADICTION)\n   201→  ⋆ SAT ∉ P\n   202→-/\n   203→\n   204→/-- Soundness for computational complexity: provability implies truth -/\n   205→def IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   206→  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   207→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n   208→    Provable sys F →\n   209→    fact\n   210→\n   211→/-- IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (Theorem 1 structure)\n   212→    This captures the VALID part of Tarnlund's argument:\n   213→    given soundness + provability + meaning, the conclusion follows. -/\n   214→theorem soundness_implies_truth :\n   215→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n   216→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n   217→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n   218→  PNotEqualsNP := by\n   219→  intro soundness provable meaning\n   220→  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n   221→\n   222→/-! ## Summary of Tarnlund's Claimed Proof Structure\n   223→\n   224→  Tarnlund's approach:\n   225→  1. ✓ Defines a formal system TheoryB' (Section 2, Axiom 1)\n   226→  2. ✓ Claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n   227→  3. ✓ Establishes computing time / proof complexity relationship (Lemma 3)\n   228→  4. ✓ Uses Haken's theorem for contradiction (Theorem 1 proof)\n   229→  5. ✗ MISSING: Proof that TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n   230→\n   231→  The gap: Without soundness, provability in the system doesn't establish\n   232→  mathematical truth. The proof proves SAT ∉ P WITHIN the formal system,\n   233→  but never shows that this formal system correctly models reality.\n   234→\n   235→  NOTE: We cannot formalize the soundness proof because it does not exist\n   236→  in Tarnlund's paper. This is the critical gap identified in the refutation.\n   237→-/\n   238→\n   239→#check soundness_implies_truth\n   240→#check PNotEqualsNP\n   241→#check IsSoundForComplexity\n   242→\n   243→end TarnlundProof\n   244→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

 179→\n   180→(* IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (valid part of argument) *)\n   181→Theorem soundness_implies_truth :\n   182→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime ->\n   183→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula ->\n   184→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP ->\n   185→  PNotEqualsNP.\n   186→Proof.\n   187→  intros soundness provable meaning.\n   188→         "content": "     1→/-\n     2→  TarnlundRefutation.lean - Refutation of Tarnlund's 2008 P≠NP attempt\n     3→\n     4→  Original paper: \"P is not equal to NP\" (arXiv:0810.5056v1, October 2008)\n     5→\n     6→  This file demonstrates WHY Tarnlund's proof attempt fails. The key insight\n     7→  is that proving a statement within a formal system does NOT establish\n     8→  mathematical truth unless the formal system is proven SOUND for that domain.\n     9→\n    10→  Specifically, the paper's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within the formal\n    11→  system TheoryB', but never establishes that TheoryB' is sound for\n    12→  computational complexity claims. Without this soundness proof, the\n    13→  derivation is meaningless for the actual P vs NP question.\n    14→\n    15→  Critique sources:\n    16→  - Henning Makholm (2008): \"Does P equal NP? This is not an answer\"\n    17→  - The formal system's axioms are not clearly specified\n    18→  - The relationship between the formal theory and actual computation\n    19→    is not rigorously established\n    20→-/\n    21→\n    22→namespace TarnlundRefutation\n    23→\n    24→/-! ## Part 1: Definitions (same as in proof/) -/\n    25→\n    26→def Language := String → Bool\n    27→def TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n    28→\n    29→def isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    30→  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n    31→\n    32→structure ClassP where\n    33→  language : Language\n    34→  decider : String → Nat\n    35→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    36→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    37→\n    38→structure ClassNP where\n    39→  language : Language\n    40→  verifier : String → String → Bool\n    41→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    42→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    43→\n    44→axiom SAT : ClassNP\n    45→\n    46→def PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    47→  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n    48→\n    49→structure Formula where\n    50→  symbols : List String\n    51→\n    52→structure FormalSystem where\n    53→  axioms : List Formula\n    54→  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n    55→\n    56→def Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n    57→  True\n    58→\n    59→def TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    60→  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n    61→\n    62→noncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n    63→\n    64→noncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n    65→  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n    66→    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n    67→\n    68→def IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True\n    69→def IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n    70→\n    71→axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n    72→\n    73→def SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n    74→  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n    75→\n    76→def FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n    77→  True\n    78→\n    79→axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n    80→\n    81→axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n    82→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n    83→\n    84→def IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n    85→  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    86→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n    87→    Provable sys F →\n    88→    fact\n    89→\n    90→/-! ## Part 2: The Critical Missing Piece\n    91→\n    92→  From Henning Makholm's critique (2008):\n    93→  \"The paper is pithy to the point of sloppiness... the formal system and\n    94→  its axioms are not clearly specified... the relationship between the formal\n    95→  theory and actual Turing machine computation is not rigorously established.\"\n    96→\n    97→  Tarnlund's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within TheoryB' (steps 46-53),\n    98→  but the crucial question is: does provability within TheoryB' mean the\n    99→  statement is actually TRUE?\n   100→\n   101→  This requires a SOUNDNESS PROOF: showing that everything provable in\n   102→  TheoryB' about computational complexity is actually true about real\n   103→  Turing machines. Tarnlund never provides this proof.\n   104→-/\n   105→\n   106→/-- A soundness proof would need to demonstrate this property exists -/\n   107→def SoundnessProof (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   108→  ∃ (_proof : Unit), IsSoundForComplexity sys\n   109→\n   110→/-- THE FATAL FLAW: No soundness proof exists in the paper.\n   111→    The paper proves things WITHIN the formal system but never shows\n   112→    the formal system correctly models computational reality. -/\n   113→axiom tarnlund_no_soundness_proof : ¬ SoundnessProof TheoryBPrime\n   114→\n   115→/-! ## Part 3: Structure of the Failed Attempt -/\n   116→\n   117→structure TarnlundAttempt where\n   118→  formalSystem : FormalSystem\n   119→  formula : Formula\n   120→  provable : Provable formalSystem formula\n   121→  consistent : IsSimplyConsistent formalSystem\n   122→\n   123→/-! ## Part 4: The Refutation\n   124→\n   125→  The refutation shows that Tarnlund's attempt has all the syntactic\n   126→  components (formal system, provability, consistency) but lacks the\n   127→  semantic component (soundness) that would connect formal derivations\n   128→  to mathematical truth.\n   129→-/\n   130→\n   131→/-- Tarnlund's attempt fails because it lacks a soundness proof -/\n   132→theorem tarnlund_fails_at_soundness :\n   133→  ∃ attempt : TarnlundAttempt,\n   134→    attempt.formalSystem = TheoryBPrime ∧\n   135→    ¬ SoundnessProof attempt.formalSystem := by\n   136→  refine ⟨⟨TheoryBPrime, SATNotInP_Formula, ?_, ?_⟩, rfl, ?_⟩\n   137→  · exact tarnlund_provability_claim\n   138→  · exact tarnlund_consistency_claim\n   139→  · exact tarnlund_no_soundness_proof\n   140→\n   141→/-- The gap: What WOULD be needed for the proof to work -/\n   142→theorem what_would_be_needed :\n   143→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n   144→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n   145→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n   146→  PNotEqualsNP := by\n   147→  intro soundness provable meaning\n   148→  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n   149→\n   150→/-! ## Part 5: Why Soundness is Hard - Counterexample\n   151→\n   152→  To illustrate why soundness matters, we construct a trivially unsound\n   153→  formal system that can \"prove\" any statement (including SAT ∉ P),\n   154→  yet clearly doesn't establish mathematical truth.\n   155→\n   156→  This demonstrates that mere provability within a system, even a\n   157→  consistent one, tells us nothing about truth without soundness.\n   158→-/\n   159→\n   160→/-- Example: An unsound formal system can \"prove\" false statements -/\n   161→def UnsoundSystem : FormalSystem :=\n   162→  { axioms := [SATNotInP_Formula],\n   163→    rules := [] }\n   164→\n   165→/-- In this trivial system, \"SAT ∉ P\" is provable (it's an axiom!) -/\n   166→theorem unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p :\n   167→  Provable UnsoundSystem SATNotInP_Formula := by\n   168→  trivial\n   169→\n   170→/-- But this doesn't make it true! The system is unsound. -/\n   171→axiom unsound_system_not_sound : ¬ IsSoundForComplexity UnsoundSystem\n   172→\n   173→/-- Moral: Provability ≠ Truth without soundness -/\n   174→theorem provability_not_truth_without_soundness :\n   175→  ∃ (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   176→    Provable sys F ∧\n   177→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ∧\n   178→    ¬ IsSoundForComplexity sys := by\n   179→  refine ⟨UnsoundSystem, SATNotInP_Formula, PNotEqualsNP, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩\n   180→  · exact unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p\n   181→  · trivial  -- Abstract meaning relation\n   182→  · exact unsound_system_not_sound\n   183→\n   184→/-! ## Summary of the Refutation\n   185→\n   186→  Tarnlund's 2008 attempt failed because it conflated TWO different concepts:\n   187→\n   188→  1. PROVABILITY within a formal system (what Tarnlund established in Theorem 1)\n   189→  2. MATHEMATICAL TRUTH (what would be needed to solve P vs NP)\n   190→\n   191→  ### The Structure of the Error (referencing the paper)\n   192→\n   193→  Tarnlund showed (Theorem 1, steps 46-53):\n   194→  - TheoryB' ⊢ \"SAT ∉ P\"  (provable in the formal system)\n   195→  - TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n   196→\n   197→  But he NEEDED to additionally show:\n   198→  - TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n   199→  - Therefore provability implies truth\n   200→  - Therefore SAT ∉ P is mathematically true\n   201→\n   202→  ### Why Soundness Cannot Be Assumed\n   203→\n   204→  Proving soundness of a formal system for computational complexity requires:\n   205→  1. Every axiom of TheoryB' is TRUE as a statement about Turing machines\n   206→  2. Every inference rule PRESERVES truth about computation\n   207→  3. The encoding of computational problems into formulas is FAITHFUL\n   208→\n   209→  Tarnlund provides none of these in the paper. As Makholm (2008) noted,\n   210→  even the axioms themselves are not clearly enough specified to verify\n   211→  whether they correctly model computation.\n   212→\n   213→  ### Lessons Learned\n   214→\n   215→  1. Formal proofs require both SYNTAX (derivations) and SEMANTICS (soundness)\n   216→  2. Provability in a system ≠ mathematical truth\n   217→  3. Soundness proofs are essential but often overlooked\n   218→  4. This error pattern appears in multiple failed P vs NP attempts\n   219→-/\n   220→\n   221→#check tarnlund_fails_at_soundness\n   222→#check what_would_be_needed\n   223→#check provability_not_truth_without_soundness\n   224→\n   225→end TarnlundRefutation\n   226→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

FormalSystem :=\n   178→  {| axioms := SATNotInP_Formula :: nil;\n   179→     rules := nil |}.\n   180→\n   181→(* In this trivial system, \"SAT ∉ P\" is provable (it's an axiom!) *)\n   182→Theorem unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p :\n   183→  Provable UnsoundSystem SATNotInP_Formula.\n   184→Proof.\n   185→  exact I.\n   186→Qed.\n   187→\n   188→(* But this doesn't make it true! The system is unsound. *)\n   189→Axiom unsound_system_not_sound : ~ IsSoundForComplexity UnsoundSystem.\n   190→\n   191→(* Moral: Provability ≠ Truth without soundness *)\n   192→Theorem provability_not_truth_without_soundness :\n   193→  exists (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   194→    Provable sys F /\\\n   195→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact /\\\n   196→    ~ IsSoundForComplexity sys.\n   197→Proof.\n   198→  exists UnsoundSystem, SATNotInP_Formula, PNotEqualsNP.\n   199→  split; [|split].\n   200→  - exact unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p.\n   201→  - exact I. (* Abstract meaning relation *)\n   202→  - exact unsound_system_not_sound.\n   203→Qed.\n   204→\n   205→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   206→(* Summary of the Refutation                                                 *)\n   207→(*                                                                           *)\n   208→(* Tarnlund's 2008 attempt failed because it conflated TWO different        *)\n   209→(* concepts:                                                                 *)\n   210→(*                                                                           *)\n   211→(* 1. PROVABILITY within a formal system (Theorem 1)                        *)\n   212→(* 2. MATHEMATICAL TRUTH (what would be needed to solve P vs NP)            *)\n   213→(*                                                                           *)\n   214→(* The Structure of the Error (referencing the paper):                       *)\n   215→(*                                                                           *)\n   216→(* Tarnlund showed (Theorem 1, steps 46-53):                                *)\n   217→(* - TheoryB' ⊢ \"SAT ∉ P\"  (provable in the formal system)                *)\n   218→(* - TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)                            *)\n   219→(*                                                                           *)\n   220→(* But he NEEDED to additionally show:                                       *)\n   221→(* - TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims                                *)\n   222→(* - Therefore provability implies truth                                     *)\n   223→(* - Therefore SAT ∉ P is mathematically true                               *)\n   224→(*                                                                           *)\n   225→(* Why Soundness Cannot Be Assumed:                                          *)\n   226→(* Proving soundness requires:                                               *)\n   227→(* 1. Every axiom of TheoryB' is TRUE about Turing machines                 *)\n   228→(* 2. Every inference rule PRESERVES truth about computation                 *)\n   229→(* 3. The encoding of computational problems into formulas is FAITHFUL      *)\n   230→(*                                                                           *)\n   231→(* Tarnlund provides none of these. As Makholm (2008) noted, even the       *)\n   232→(* axioms are not clearly enough specified to verify correctness.           *)\n   233→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   234→\n   235→(* Verification checks *)\n   236→Check tarnlund_fails_at_soundness.\n   237→Check what_would_be_needed.\n   238→Check provability_not_truth_without_soundness.\n   239→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

claim:\n   114→                return \"✓\"  # P=NP claim\n   115→            elif \"p≠np\" in claim or \"pneqnp\" in claim or \"p!=np\" in claim or \"p ≠ np\" in claim:\n   116→                return folder_name = self.path.name.lower()\n   121→        if \"peqnp\" in folder_name:\n   122→            return \"✓\"\n   123→        elif \"pneqnp\" in folder_name:\n   124→            return \"✗\"\n   125→        elif \"unprovable\" in folder_name:\n   126→            return \"?\"\n   127→        return \"-\"\n   128→\n   129→\n   130→def parse_metadata_from_readme(readme_path: Path) -> Optional[AttemptMetadata]:\n   131→    \"\"\"Extract metadata from README.md file.\"\"\"\n   132→    if not readme_path.exists():\n   133→        return None\n   134→\n   135→    try:\n   136→ 'attempt_id': r'\\*\\*Attempt ID\\*\\*[:\\s]*(\\d+)',\n   154→    }\n   155→\n   156→    for fld, pattern in patterns.items():\n   157→        match = re.search(pattern, content, re.IGNORECASE)\n   158→        if match:\n   159→            setattr(metadata, fld, match.group(1).strip())\n   160→\n   161→    # Try to extract claim from folder name if not found\n   162→    if not metadata.claim:\n   163→        folder_name = readme_path.parent.name.lower()\n   164→        if \"peqnp\" in folder_name:\n   165→            metadata.claim = \"P = NP\"\n   166→        elif \"pneqnp\" in \"\"\"Validate the structure of a single attempt directory.\"\"\"\n   182→    result = StructureValidation(path=attempt_path)\n   183→\n   184→    # Check main README\n   185→    readme_path = Haken's theorem on resolution proof complexity of pigeonhole formulas\n    13→\n    14→  This formalization captures what Tarnlund CLAIMED, showing each step of the\n    15→  original paper as Lean definitions and axioms.\n    16→-/\n    17→\n    18→namespace TarnlundProof\n    19→\n    20→/-! ## Section 1: Introduction - Overview of the Argument\n    21→\n    22→  From the paper (Section 1):\n    23→  \"SAT ∉ P is true, theorem 1, and provable, corollary 9, in a simply consistent\n    24→  extension B' of a first order theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom\n    25→  B characterizing a universal Turing machine.\"\n    26→-/\n    27→\n    28→/-! ## Section 2: A Theory of Computing\n    29→\n    30→  From the paper (Section 2):\n    31→  \"Before applying the axiomatic method to computing complexity, a first order\n    32→  theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal\n    33→  Turing machine, is presented.\"\n    34→-/\n    35→\n    36→/-- Binary strings as inputs -/\n    37→def Language := String → Bool\n    38→\n    39→/-- Time complexity function -/\n    40→def TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n    41→\n    42→/-- Polynomial time (Definition 8: p(a) for c·|a|^q some c q ∈ ℕ) -/\n    43→def isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    44→  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n    45→\n    46→/-- Class P: polynomial-time decidable languages -/\n    47→structure ClassP where\n    48→  language : Language\n    49→  decider : String → Nat\n    50→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    51→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    52→\n    53→/-- Class NP: polynomial-time verifiable languages -/\n    54→structure ClassNP where\n    55→  language : Language\n    56→  verifier : String → String → Bool\n    57→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    58→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    59→\n    60→/-- SAT problem (axiomatically NP-complete) -/\n    61→axiom SAT : ClassNP\n    62→\n    63→/-- P ≠ NP statement -/\n    64→def PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    65→  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n    66→\n    67→/-! ## Section 2: Formal System Definitions\n    68→\n    69→  From the paper:\n    70→  \"Syntactically, there are two predicate symbols of B written T(i, a, u), and\n    71→  U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u).\"\n    72→-/\n    73→\n    74→/-- A formula in the formal system (Definition 3) -/\n    75→structure Formula where\n    76→  symbols : List String\n    77→\n    78→/-- A formal system with axioms and inference rules -/\n    79→structure FormalSystem where\n    80→  axioms : List Formula\n    81→  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n    82→\n    83→/-- Provability in a formal system -/\n    84→def Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n    85→  True  -- Abstract provability relation\n    86→\n    87→/-! ## Theory B and Axiom B (Axiom 1)\n    88→\n    89→  From the paper (Axiom 1):\n    90→  \"B for ∀ T(i, a, u) ⊃ U(∅, ∅, a.∅, 1, i, i, u) ...\"\n    91→  The axiom has six sentences (17)-(22) characterizing a universal Turing machine.\n    92→-/\n    93→\n    94→/-- Theory B (Tarnlund's base formal system) -/\n    95→def TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    96→  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n    97→\n    98→/-- Universal Turing Machine axiom (Axiom 1, sentences 17-22) -/\n    99→noncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n   100→\n   101→/-- Theory B' (Theory B + Universal TM axiom, simply consistent extension) -/\n   102→noncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n   103→  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n   104→    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n   105→\n   106→/-! ## Section 2: Consistency Claims\n   107→\n   108→  From the paper (Corollary 2):\n   109→  \"Theory B is simply consistent in U i.e., there is no contradiction\"\n   110→-/\n   111→\n   112→/-- Consistency of a formal system -/\n   113→def IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True  -- Abstract consistency\n   114→\n   115→/-- Simple consistency (Definition from Kleene 1967) -/\n   116→def IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n   117→\n   118→/-- Tarnlund claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2) -/\n   119→axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n   120→\n   121→/-! ## Section 2: Lemma 1\n   122→\n   123→  From the paper:\n   124→  \"Lemma 1: ∃uT(i, a, u) ⊃ ⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u) any i ∈ M a ∈ L.\"\n   125→  If a computation terminates, it is provable in theory B.\n   126→-/\n   127→\n   128→/-- Lemma 1: True computations are provable in theory B -/\n   129→axiom lemma1_computations_provable :\n   130→  ∀ (F : Formula), Provable TheoryBPrime F\n   131→\n   132→/-! ## Section 3: Computing Time (Definitions 6-15)\n   133→\n   134→  From the paper (Definition 15):\n   135→  \"SAT ∈ P for ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, F.∅, u) in p(F) some i ∈ U any F ∈ F.\"\n   136→-/\n   137→\n   138→/-- Formula representing \"SAT ∉ P\" -/\n   139→def SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n   140→  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n   141→\n   142→/-- What it means for a formula to represent a computational fact -/\n   143→def FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n   144→  True  -- Abstract meaning relation\n   145→\n   146→/-- Tarnlund claims to prove \"SAT ∉ P\" in TheoryB' (Theorem 1) -/\n   147→axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n   148→\n   149→/-- The formula is claimed to mean P ≠ NP -/\n   150→axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n   151→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n   152→\n   153→/-! ## Section 4: Computing Time and Proof Complexity\n   154→\n   155→  From the paper (Lemma 3):\n   156→  \"⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F) ⊃ Y(i, F, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|F|^q\"\n   157→\n   158→  This is the principal lemma establishing the relationship between\n   159→  computing time and proof complexity.\n   160→-/\n   161→\n   162→/-- Y(i, F, n) formula - Definition 20\n   163→    Y(i, F, n) for (Q₀ ⊃ F) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁)\n   164→    This represents the propositional formula that relates computing time\n   165→    to a chain of implications leading to the tautology F. -/\n   166→def Y_formula (_i : Nat) (_F : Formula) (_n : Nat) : Prop := True\n   167→\n   168→/-- Lemma 3 (Principal Lemma):\n   169→    If computing time is polynomial, then Y(i, F, n) holds with polynomial n.\n   170→    This is the key claim linking computing time to proof complexity. -/\n   171→axiom lemma3_computing_time_proof_complexity :\n   172→  ∀ (i : Nat) (F : Formula) (n : Nat),\n   173→    Y_formula i F n\n   174→\n   175→/-! ## Section 4.1: Proof Complexity and Haken's Theorem\n   176→\n   177→  From the paper:\n   178→  \"Every resolution proof of PFₙ contains at least cⁿ different clauses\n   179→  for c > 1 some c ∈ ℝ any sufficiently large n ∈ ℕ\" (Haken 1985)\n   180→-/\n   181→\n   182→/-- Resolution provability (Definition 22) -/\n   183→def ResolutionProvable (_F : Formula) (_size : Nat) : Prop := True\n   184→\n   185→/-- Haken's theorem: resolution proofs of pigeonhole formulas require\n   186→    exponentially many clauses -/\n   187→axiom hakens_theorem :\n   188→  ∀ (m : Nat), ∃ (minSize : Nat),\n   189→    (∀ (k : Nat), minSize > k * m ^ k) ∧\n   190→    ∀ (size : Nat), size < minSize → ¬ ResolutionProvable\n   191→      { symbols := [\"PF\", toString m] } size\n   192→\n   193→/-! ## Main Results: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2\n   194→\n   195→  From the paper (Theorem 1 proof, steps 46-53):\n   196→  ⋆ SAT ∈ P (assumption for contradiction)\n   197→  ⋆ ⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F)  (Corollary 5)\n   198→  ⋆ Y(i, PFₘ, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|PFₘ|^q  (Lemma 3)\n   199→  ⋆ ⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ)  (from Y formula)\n   200→  ⋆ ¬(⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ))  (Haken's theorem - CONTRADICTION)\n   201→  ⋆ SAT ∉ P\n   202→-/\n   203→\n   204→/-- Soundness for computational complexity: provability implies truth -/\n   205→def IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   206→  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   207→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n   208→    Provable sys F →\n   209→    fact\n   210→\n   211→/-- IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (Theorem 1 structure)\n   212→    This captures the VALID part of Tarnlund's argument:\n   213→    given soundness + provability + meaning, the conclusion follows. -/\n   214→theorem soundness_implies_truth :\n   215→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n   216→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n   217→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n   218→  PNotEqualsNP := by\n   219→  intro soundness provable meaning\n   220→  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n   221→\n   222→/-! ## Summary of Tarnlund's Claimed Proof Structure\n   223→\n   224→  Tarnlund's approach:\n   225→  1. ✓ Defines a formal system TheoryB' (Section 2, Axiom 1)\n   226→  2. ✓ Claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n   227→  3. ✓ Establishes computing time / proof complexity relationship (Lemma 3)\n   228→  4. ✓ Uses Haken's theorem for contradiction (Theorem 1 proof)\n   229→  5. ✗ MISSING: Proof that TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n   230→\n   231→  The gap: Without soundness, provability in the system doesn't establish\n   232→  mathematical truth. The proof proves SAT ∉ P WITHIN the formal system,\n   233→  but never shows that this formal system correctly models reality.\n   234→\n   235→  NOTE: We cannot formalize the soundness proof because it does not exist\n   236→  in Tarnlund's paper. This is the critical gap identified in the refutation.\n   237→-/\n   238→\n   239→#check soundness_implies_truth\n   240→#check PNotEqualsNP\n   241→#check IsSoundForComplexity\n   242→\n   243→end TarnlundProof\n   244→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"
