        "content": "     1→/-\n     2→  TarnlundRefutation.lean - Refutation of Tarnlund's 2008 P≠NP attempt\n     3→\n     4→  Original paper: \"P is not equal to NP\" (arXiv:0810.5056v1, October 2008)\n     5→\n     6→  This file demonstrates WHY Tarnlund's proof attempt fails. The key insight\n     7→  is that proving a statement within a formal system does NOT establish\n     8→  mathematical truth unless the formal system is proven SOUND for that domain.\n     9→\n    10→  Specifically, the paper's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within the formal\n    11→  system TheoryB', but never establishes that TheoryB' is sound for\n    12→  computational complexity claims. Without this soundness proof, the\n    13→  derivation is meaningless for the actual P vs NP question.\n    14→\n    15→  Critique sources:\n    16→  - Henning Makholm (2008): \"Does P equal NP? This is not an answer\"\n    17→  - The formal system's axioms are not clearly specified\n    18→  - The relationship between the formal theory and actual computation\n    19→    is not rigorously established\n    20→-/\n    21→\n    22→namespace TarnlundRefutation\n    23→\n    24→/-! ## Part 1: Definitions (same as in proof/) -/\n    25→\n    26→def Language := String → Bool\n    27→def TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n    28→\n    29→def isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    30→  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n    31→\n    32→structure ClassP where\n    33→  language : Language\n    34→  decider : String → Nat\n    35→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    36→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    37→\n    38→structure ClassNP where\n    39→  language : Language\n    40→  verifier : String → String → Bool\n    41→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    42→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    43→\n    44→axiom SAT : ClassNP\n    45→\n    46→def PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    47→  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n    48→\n    49→structure Formula where\n    50→  symbols : List String\n    51→\n    52→structure FormalSystem where\n    53→  axioms : List Formula\n    54→  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n    55→\n    56→def Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n    57→  True\n    58→\n    59→def TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    60→  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n    61→\n    62→noncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n    63→\n    64→noncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n    65→  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n    66→    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n    67→\n    68→def IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True\n    69→def IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n    70→\n    71→axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n    72→\n    73→def SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n    74→  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n    75→\n    76→def FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n    77→  True\n    78→\n    79→axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n    80→\n    81→axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n    82→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n    83→\n    84→def IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n    85→  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    86→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n    87→    Provable sys F →\n    88→    fact\n    89→\n    90→/-! ## Part 2: The Critical Missing Piece\n    91→\n    92→  From Henning Makholm's critique (2008):\n    93→  \"The paper is pithy to the point of sloppiness... the formal system and\n    94→  its axioms are not clearly specified... the relationship between the formal\n    95→  theory and actual Turing machine computation is not rigorously established.\"\n    96→\n    97→  Tarnlund's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within TheoryB' (steps 46-53),\n    98→  but the crucial question is: does provability within TheoryB' mean the\n    99→  statement is actually TRUE?\n   100→\n   101→  This requires a SOUNDNESS PROOF: showing that everything provable in\n   102→  TheoryB' about computational complexity is actually true about real\n   103→  Turing machines. Tarnlund never provides this proof.\n   104→-/\n   105→\n   106→/-- A soundness proof would need to demonstrate this property exists -/\n   107→def SoundnessProof (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   108→  ∃ (_proof : Unit), IsSoundForComplexity sys\n   109→\n   110→/-- THE FATAL FLAW: No soundness proof exists in the paper.\n   111→    The paper proves things WITHIN the formal system but never shows\n   112→    the formal system correctly models computational reality. -/\n   113→axiom tarnlund_no_soundness_proof : ¬ SoundnessProof TheoryBPrime\n   114→\n   115→/-! ## Part 3: Structure of the Failed Attempt -/\n   116→\n   117→structure TarnlundAttempt where\n   118→  formalSystem : FormalSystem\n   119→  formula : Formula\n   120→  provable : Provable formalSystem formula\n   121→  consistent : IsSimplyConsistent formalSystem\n   122→\n   123→/-! ## Part 4: The Refutation\n   124→\n   125→  The refutation shows that Tarnlund's attempt has all the syntactic\n   126→  components (formal system, provability, consistency) but lacks the\n   127→  semantic component (soundness) that would connect formal derivations\n   128→  to mathematical truth.\n   129→-/\n   130→\n   131→/-- Tarnlund's attempt fails because it lacks a soundness proof -/\n   132→theorem tarnlund_fails_at_soundness :\n   133→  ∃ attempt : TarnlundAttempt,\n   134→    attempt.formalSystem = TheoryBPrime ∧\n   135→    ¬ SoundnessProof attempt.formalSystem := by\n   136→  refine ⟨⟨TheoryBPrime, SATNotInP_Formula, ?_, ?_⟩, rfl, ?_⟩\n   137→  · exact tarnlund_provability_claim\n   138→  · exact tarnlund_consistency_claim\n   139→  · exact tarnlund_no_soundness_proof\n   140→\n   141→/-- The gap: What WOULD be needed for the proof to work -/\n   142→theorem what_would_be_needed :\n   143→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n   144→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n   145→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n   146→  PNotEqualsNP := by\n   147→  intro soundness provable meaning\n   148→  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n   149→\n   150→/-! ## Part 5: Why Soundness is Hard - Counterexample\n   151→\n   152→  To illustrate why soundness matters, we construct a trivially unsound\n   153→  formal system that can \"prove\" any statement (including SAT ∉ P),\n   154→  yet clearly doesn't establish mathematical truth.\n   155→\n   156→  This demonstrates that mere provability within a system, even a\n   157→  consistent one, tells us nothing about truth without soundness.\n   158→-/\n   159→\n   160→/-- Example: An unsound formal system can \"prove\" false statements -/\n   161→def UnsoundSystem : FormalSystem :=\n   162→  { axioms := [SATNotInP_Formula],\n   163→    rules := [] }\n   164→\n   165→/-- In this trivial system, \"SAT ∉ P\" is provable (it's an axiom!) -/\n   166→theorem unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p :\n   167→  Provable UnsoundSystem SATNotInP_Formula := by\n   168→  trivial\n   169→\n   170→/-- But this doesn't make it true! The system is unsound. -/\n   171→axiom unsound_system_not_sound : ¬ IsSoundForComplexity UnsoundSystem\n   172→\n   173→/-- Moral: Provability ≠ Truth without soundness -/\n   174→theorem provability_not_truth_without_soundness :\n   175→  ∃ (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   176→    Provable sys F ∧\n   177→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ∧\n   178→    ¬ IsSoundForComplexity sys := by\n   179→  refine ⟨UnsoundSystem, SATNotInP_Formula, PNotEqualsNP, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩\n   180→  · exact unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p\n   181→  · trivial  -- Abstract meaning relation\n   182→  · exact unsound_system_not_sound\n   183→\n   184→/-! ## Summary of the Refutation\n   185→\n   186→  Tarnlund's 2008 attempt failed because it conflated TWO different concepts:\n   187→\n   188→  1. PROVABILITY within a formal system (what Tarnlund established in Theorem 1)\n   189→  2. MATHEMATICAL TRUTH (what would be needed to solve P vs NP)\n   190→\n   191→  ### The Structure of the Error (referencing the paper)\n   192→\n   193→  Tarnlund showed (Theorem 1, steps 46-53):\n   194→  - TheoryB' ⊢ \"SAT ∉ P\"  (provable in the formal system)\n   195→  - TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n   196→\n   197→  But he NEEDED to additionally show:\n   198→  - TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n   199→  - Therefore provability implies truth\n   200→  - Therefore SAT ∉ P is mathematically true\n   201→\n   202→  ### Why Soundness Cannot Be Assumed\n   203→\n   204→  Proving soundness of a formal system for computational complexity requires:\n   205→  1. Every axiom of TheoryB' is TRUE as a statement about Turing machines\n   206→  2. Every inference rule PRESERVES truth about computation\n   207→  3. The encoding of computational problems into formulas is FAITHFUL\n   208→\n   209→  Tarnlund provides none of these in the paper. As Makholm (2008) noted,\n   210→  even the axioms themselves are not clearly enough specified to verify\n   211→  whether they correctly model computation.\n   212→\n   213→  ### Lessons Learned\n   214→\n   215→  1. Formal proofs require both SYNTAX (derivations) and SEMANTICS (soundness)\n   216→  2. Provability in a system ≠ mathematical truth\n   217→  3. Soundness proofs are essential but often overlooked\n   218→  4. This error pattern appears in multiple failed P vs NP attempts\n   219→-/\n   220→\n   221→#check tarnlund_fails_at_soundness\n   222→#check what_would_be_needed\n   223→#check provability_not_truth_without_soundness\n   224→\n   225→end TarnlundRefutation\n   226→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

A first-order theory B with axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine\n    12→  2. A relationship between computing time and proof complexity (Lemma 3)\n    13→  3. Haken's theorem on resolution proof complexity of pigeonhole formulas\n    14→*)\n    15→\n    16→From Stdlib Require Import String List Nat.\n    17→\n    18→Open Scope string_scope.\n    19→\n    20→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    21→(* Section 2: A Theory of Computing                                          *)\n    22→(* From the paper: \"Before applying the axiomatic method to computing        *)\n    23→(* complexity, a first order theory B of computing, with a single finite     *)\n    24→(* axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine, is presented.\"         *)\n    25→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    26→\n    27→Definition Language := string -> bool.\n    28→Definition TimeComplexity := nat -> nat.\n    29→\n    30→(* Definition 8: p(a) for c·|a|^q some c q ∈ ℕ *)\n    31→Definition isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    32→  exists (c k : nat), forall n : nat, T n <= c * n ^ k.\n    33→\n    34→(* Class P: polynomial-time decidable languages *)\n    35→Record ClassP : Type := mkClassP {\n    36→  p_language : Language;\n    37→  p_decider : string -> nat;\n    38→  p_timeComplexity : TimeComplexity;\n    39→  p_isPoly : isPolynomial p_timeComplexity\n    40→}.\n    41→\n    42→(* Class NP: polynomial-time verifiable languages *)\n    43→Record ClassNP : Type := mkClassNP {\n    44→  np_language : Language;\n    45→  np_verifier : string -> string -> bool;\n    46→  np_timeComplexity : TimeComplexity;\n    47→  np_isPoly : isPolynomial np_timeComplexity\n    48→}.\n    49→\n    50→Axiom SAT : ClassNP.\n    51→\n    52→(* P ≠ NP statement *)\n    53→Definition PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    54→  forall (p : ClassP), exists (s : string), np_language SAT s <> p_language p s.\n    55→\n    56→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    57→(* Section 2: Formal System Definitions                                      *)\n    58→(* From the paper: \"Syntactically, there are two predicate symbols of B      *)\n    59→(* written T(i, a, u), and U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u).\"                        *)\n    60→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    61→\n    62→(* Definition 3: Formulas of theory B *)\n    63→Record Formula : Type := mkFormula {\n    64→  symbols : list string;\n    65→  wellFormed : True\n    66→}.\n    67→\n    68→(* A formal system with axioms and inference rules *)\n    69→Record FormalSystem : Type := mkFormalSystem {\n    70→  axioms : list Formula;\n    71→  rules : list (list Formula -> Formula)\n    72→}.\n    73→\n    74→(* Provability in a formal system *)\n    75→Definition Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) : Prop := True.\n    76→\n    77→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    78→(* Theory B and Axiom B (Axiom 1)                                           *)\n    79→(* From the paper (Axiom 1): \"B for ∀ T(i,a,u) ⊃ U(∅,∅,a.∅,1,i,i,u) ...\" *)\n    80→(* Six sentences (17)-(22) characterizing a universal Turing machine         *)\n    81→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    82→\n    83→Definition TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    84→  {| axioms := nil; rules := nil |}.\n    85→\n    86→(* Universal Turing Machine axiom (Axiom 1, sentences 17-22) *)\n    87→Axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula.\n    88→\n    89→(* Theory B' (Theory B + Universal TM axiom, simply consistent extension) *)\n    90→Definition TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n    91→  {| axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: axioms TheoryB;\n    92→     rules := rules TheoryB |}.\n    93→\n    94→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    95→(* Section 2: Consistency Claims                                             *)\n    96→(* From the paper (Corollary 2):                                             *)\n    97→(* \"Theory B is simply consistent in U i.e., there is no contradiction\"     *)\n    98→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    99→\n   100→Definition IsConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True.\n   101→Definition IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys.\n   102→\n   103→(* Corollary 2: Tarnlund claims TheoryB' is simply consistent *)\n   104→Axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime.\n   105→\n   106→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   107→(* Section 2: Lemma 1                                                        *)\n   108→(* \"Lemma 1: ∃uT(i,a,u) ⊃ ⊢ B → ∃uT(i,a,u) any i ∈ M a ∈ L.\"           *)\n   109→(* If a computation terminates, it is provable in theory B.                  *)\n   110→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   111→\n   112→Axiom lemma1_computations_provable :\n   113→  forall (F : Formula), Provable TheoryBPrime F.\n   114→\n   115→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   116→(* Section 3: Computing Time (Definitions 6-15)                              *)\n   117→(* Definition 15: SAT ∈ P for ⊢ B → ∃u T(i,F.∅,u) in p(F)                *)\n   118→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   119→\n   120→Definition SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n   121→  mkFormula (\"SAT\" :: \"not\" :: \"in\" :: \"P\" :: nil) I.\n   122→\n   123→Definition FormulaMeansComputationalFact (F : Formula) (fact : Prop) : Prop := True.\n   124→\n   125→(* Theorem 1: Tarnlund claims to prove \"SAT ∉ P\" in TheoryB' *)\n   126→Axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula.\n   127→\n   128→(* The formula is claimed to mean P ≠ NP *)\n   129→Axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim : FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP.\n   130→\n   131→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   132→(* Section 4: Computing Time and Proof Complexity                            *)\n   133→(* Lemma 3 (Principal Lemma):                                               *)\n   134→(* \"⊢ B → T(i,¬F.∅,∅.1) in p(F) ⊃ Y(i,F,n) ∧ n ≤ c·|F|^q\"             *)\n   135→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   136→\n   137→(* Definition 20: Y(i, F, n) formula\n   138→   Y(i,F,n) for (Q₀ ⊃ F) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁) *)\n   139→Definition Y_formula (i : nat) (F : Formula) (n : nat) : Prop := True.\n   140→\n   141→(* Lemma 3: Key claim linking computing time to proof complexity *)\n   142→Axiom lemma3_computing_time_proof_complexity :\n   143→  forall (i : nat) (F : Formula) (n : nat),\n   144→    Y_formula i F n.\n   145→\n   146→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   147→(* Section 4.1: Proof Complexity and Haken's Theorem                         *)\n   148→(* \"Every resolution proof of PFₙ contains at least cⁿ different clauses   *)\n   149→(* for c > 1 some c ∈ ℝ any sufficiently large n ∈ ℕ\" (Haken 1985)        *)\n   150→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   151→\n   152→(* Definition 22: Resolution provability *)\n   153→Definition ResolutionProvable (F : Formula) (size : nat) : Prop := True.\n   154→\n   155→(* Haken's theorem *)\n   156→Axiom hakens_theorem :\n   157→  forall (m : nat), exists (minSize : nat),\n   158→    (forall (k : nat), minSize > k * m ^ k) /\\\n   159→    forall (size : nat), size < minSize ->\n   160→      ~ ResolutionProvable (mkFormula (\"PF\" :: nil) I) size.\n   161→\n   162→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   163→(* Main Results: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2                                     *)\n   164→(* Theorem 1 proof (steps 46-53):                                           *)\n   165→(* ⋆ SAT ∈ P (assumption for contradiction)                                 *)\n   166→(* ⋆ ⊢ B → T(i,¬F.∅,∅.1) in p(F)  (Corollary 5)                          *)\n   167→(* ⋆ Y(i,PFₘ,n) ∧ n ≤ c·|PFₘ|^q  (Lemma 3)                              *)\n   168→(* ⋆ ⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ)  (from Y formula)                                  *)\n   169→(* ⋆ ¬(⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ))  (Haken's theorem - CONTRADICTION)              *)\n   170→(* ⋆ SAT ∉ P                                                                *)\n   171→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   172→\n   173→(* Soundness for computational complexity: provability implies truth *)\n   174→Definition IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   175→  forall (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   176→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ->\n   177→    Provable sys F ->\n   178→    fact.\n   179→\n   180→(* IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (valid part of argument) *)\n   181→Theorem soundness_implies_truth :\n   182→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime ->\n   183→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula ->\n   184→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP ->\n   185→  PNotEqualsNP.\n   186→Proof.\n   187→  intros soundness provable meaning.\n   188→  exact (soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable).\n   189→Qed.\n   190→\n   191→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   192→(* Summary of Tarnlund's Claimed Proof Structure                             *)\n   193→(*                                                                           *)\n   194→(* Tarnlund's approach:                                                      *)\n   195→(* 1. ✓ Defines a formal system TheoryB' (Section 2, Axiom 1)               *)\n   196→(* 2. ✓ Claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)                  *)\n   197→(* 3. ✓ Establishes computing time / proof complexity relationship (Lemma 3)*)\n   198→(* 4. ✓ Uses Haken's theorem for contradiction (Theorem 1 proof)            *)\n   199→(* 5. ✗ MISSING: Proof that TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims         *)\n   200→(*                                                                           *)\n   201→(* The gap: Without soundness, provability in the system doesn't establish  *)\n   202→(* mathematical truth. The proof proves SAT ∉ P WITHIN the formal system,  *)\n   203→(* but never shows that this formal system correctly models reality.        *)\n   204→(*                                                                           *)\n   205→(* NOTE: We cannot formalize the soundness proof because it does not exist  *)\n   206→(* in Tarnlund's paper. This is the critical gap identified in the          *)\n   207→(* refutation.                                                               *)\n   208→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   209→\n   210→Check soundness_implies_truth.\n   211→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware.         "content": "     1→(*\n     2→  TarnlundRefutation.v - Refutation of Tarnlund's 2008 P≠NP attempt\n     3→\n     4→  Original paper: \"P is not equal to NP\" (arXiv:0810.5056v1, October 2008)\n     5→\n     6→  This file demonstrates WHY Tarnlund's proof attempt fails. The key insight\n     7→  is that proving a statement within a formal system does NOT establish\n     8→  mathematical truth unless the formal system is proven SOUND for that domain.\n     9→\n    10→  Specifically, the paper's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within the formal\n    11→  system TheoryB', but never establishes that TheoryB' is sound for\n    12→  computational complexity claims.\n    13→\n    14→  Critique sources:\n    15→  - Henning Makholm (2008): \"Does P equal NP? This is not an answer\"\n    16→  - The formal system's axioms are not clearly specified\n    17→  - The relationship between the formal theory and actual computation\n    18→    is not rigorously established\n    19→*)\n    20→\n    21→From Stdlib Require Import String List Nat.\n    22→\n    23→Open Scope string_scope.\n    24→\n    25→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    26→(* Part 1: Definitions (same as in proof/)                                   *)\n    27→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    28→\n    29→Definition Language := string -> bool.\n    30→Definition TimeComplexity := nat -> nat.\n    31→\n    32→Definition isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    33→  exists (c k : nat), forall n : nat, T n <= c * n ^ k.\n    34→\n    35→Record ClassP : Type := mkClassP {\n    36→  p_language : Language;\n    37→  p_decider : string -> nat;\n    38→  p_timeComplexity : TimeComplexity;\n    39→  p_isPoly : isPolynomial p_timeComplexity\n    40→}.\n    41→\n    42→Record ClassNP : Type := mkClassNP {\n    43→  np_language : Language;\n    44→  np_verifier : string -> string -> bool;\n    45→  np_timeComplexity : TimeComplexity;\n    46→  np_isPoly : isPolynomial np_timeComplexity\n    47→}.\n    48→\n    49→Axiom SAT : ClassNP.\n    50→\n    51→Definition PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    52→  forall (p : ClassP), exists (s : string), np_language SAT s <> p_language p s.\n    53→\n    54→Record Formula : Type := mkFormula {\n    55→  symbols : list string;\n    56→  wellFormed : True\n    57→}.\n    58→\n    59→Record FormalSystem : Type := mkFormalSystem {\n    60→  axioms : list Formula;\n    61→  rules : list (list Formula -> Formula)\n    62→}.\n    63→\n    64→Definition Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) : Prop := True.\n    65→\n    66→Definition TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    67→  {| axioms := nil; rules := nil |}.\n    68→\n    69→Axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula.\n    70→\n    71→Definition TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n    72→  {| axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: axioms TheoryB;\n    73→     rules := rules TheoryB |}.\n    74→\n    75→Definition IsConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True.\n    76→Definition IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys.\n    77→\n    78→Axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime.\n    79→\n    80→Definition SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n    81→  mkFormula (\"SAT\" :: \"not\" :: \"in\" :: \"P\" :: nil) I.\n    82→\n    83→Definition FormulaMeansComputationalFact (F : Formula) (fact : Prop) : Prop := True.\n    84→\n    85→Axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula.\n    86→Axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim : FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP.\n    87→\n    88→Definition IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n    89→  forall (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    90→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ->\n    91→    Provable sys F ->\n    92→    fact.\n    93→\n    94→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    95→(* Part 2: The Critical Missing Piece                                        *)\n    96→(*                                                                           *)\n    97→(* From Henning Makholm's critique (2008):                                   *)\n    98→(* \"The paper is pithy to the point of sloppiness... the formal system and  *)\n    99→(* its axioms are not clearly specified... the relationship between the      *)\n   100→(* formal theory and actual Turing machine computation is not rigorously    *)\n   101→(* established.\"                                                             *)\n   102→(*                                                                           *)\n   103→(* Tarnlund's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within TheoryB' (steps 46-53),   *)\n   104→(* but the crucial question is: does provability within TheoryB' mean the   *)\n   105→(* statement is actually TRUE?                                               *)\n   106→(*                                                                           *)\n   107→(* This requires a SOUNDNESS PROOF: showing that everything provable in     *)\n   108→(* TheoryB' about computational complexity is actually true about real      *)\n   109→(* Turing machines. Tarnlund never provides this proof.                     *)\n   110→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   111→\n   112→(* A soundness proof would need to demonstrate this property exists *)\n   113→Definition SoundnessProof (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   114→  exists (_proof : unit), IsSoundForComplexity sys.\n   115→\n   116→(* THE FATAL FLAW: No soundness proof exists in the paper.\n   117→   The paper proves things WITHIN the formal system but never shows\n   118→   the formal system correctly models computational reality. *)\n   119→Axiom tarnlund_no_soundness_proof : ~ SoundnessProof TheoryBPrime.\n   120→\n   121→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   122→(* Part 3: Structure of Tarnlund's Attempt                                   *)\n   123→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   124→\n   125→Record TarnlundAttempt : Type := mkTarnlundAttempt {\n   126→  ta_formalSystem : FormalSystem;\n   127→  ta_formula : Formula;\n   128→  ta_provable : Provable ta_formalSystem ta_formula;\n   129→  ta_consistent : IsSimplyConsistent ta_formalSystem\n   130→}.\n   131→\n   132→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   133→(* Part 4: The Refutation                                                    *)\n   134→(*                                                                           *)\n   135→(* The refutation shows that Tarnlund's attempt has all the syntactic       *)\n   136→(* components (formal system, provability, consistency) but lacks the        *)\n   137→(* semantic component (soundness) that would connect formal derivations      *)\n   138→(* to mathematical truth.                                                    *)\n   139→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   140→\n   141→(* Tarnlund's attempt fails because it lacks a soundness proof *)\n   142→Theorem tarnlund_fails_at_soundness :\n   143→  exists attempt : TarnlundAttempt,\n   144→    ta_formalSystem attempt = TheoryBPrime /\\\n   145→    ~ SoundnessProof (ta_formalSystem attempt).\n   146→Proof.\n   147→  exists (mkTarnlundAttempt\n   148→    TheoryBPrime\n   149→    SATNotInP_Formula\n   150→    tarnlund_provability_claim\n   151→    tarnlund_consistency_claim).\n   152→  split.\n   153→  - reflexivity.\n   154→  - exact tarnlund_no_soundness_proof.\n   155→Qed.\n   156→\n   157→(* The gap: What WOULD be needed for the proof to work *)\n   158→Theorem what_would_be_needed :\n   159→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime ->\n   160→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula ->\n   161→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP ->\n   162→  PNotEqualsNP.\n   163→Proof.\n   164→  intros soundness provable meaning.\n   165→  exact (soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable).\n   166→Qed.\n   167→\n   168→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   169→(* Part 5: Why Soundness is Hard - Counterexample                            *)\n   170→(*                                                                           *)\n   171→(* To illustrate why soundness matters, we construct a trivially unsound    *)\n   172→(* formal system that can \"prove\" any statement (including SAT ∉ P),       *)\n   173→(* yet clearly doesn't establish mathematical truth.                        *)\n   174→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   175→\n   176→(* Example: An unsound formal system can \"prove\" false statements *)\n   177→Definition UnsoundSystem : FormalSystem :=\n   178→  {| axioms := SATNotInP_Formula :: nil;\n   179→     rules := nil |}.\n   180→\n   181→(* In this trivial system, \"SAT ∉ P\" is provable (it's an axiom!) *)\n   182→Theorem unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p :\n   183→  Provable UnsoundSystem SATNotInP_Formula.\n   184→Proof.\n   185→  exact I.\n   186→Qed.\n   187→\n   188→(* But this doesn't make it true! The system is unsound. *)\n   189→Axiom unsound_system_not_sound : ~ IsSoundForComplexity UnsoundSystem.\n   190→\n   191→(* Moral: Provability ≠ Truth without soundness *)\n   192→Theorem provability_not_truth_without_soundness :\n   193→  exists (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   194→    Provable sys F /\\\n   195→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact /\\\n   196→    ~ IsSoundForComplexity sys.\n   197→Proof.\n   198→  exists UnsoundSystem, SATNotInP_Formula, PNotEqualsNP.\n   199→  split; [|split].\n   200→  - exact unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p.\n   201→  - exact I. (* Abstract meaning relation *)\n   202→  - exact unsound_system_not_sound.\n   203→Qed.\n   204→\n   205→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   206→(* Summary of the Refutation                                                 *)\n   207→(*                                                                           *)\n   208→(* Tarnlund's 2008 attempt failed because it conflated TWO different        *)\n   209→(* concepts:                                                                 *)\n   210→(*                                                                           *)\n   211→(* 1. PROVABILITY within a formal system (Theorem 1)                        *)\n   212→(* 2. MATHEMATICAL TRUTH (what would be needed to solve P vs NP)            *)\n   213→(*                                                                           *)\n   214→(* The Structure of the Error (referencing the paper):                       *)\n   215→(*                                                                           *)\n   216→(* Tarnlund showed (Theorem 1, steps 46-53):                                *)\n   217→(* - TheoryB' ⊢ \"SAT ∉ P\"  (provable in the formal system)                *)\n   218→(* - TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)                            *)\n   219→(*                                                                           *)\n   220→(* But he NEEDED to additionally show:                                       *)\n   221→(* - TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims                                *)\n   222→(* - Therefore provability implies truth                                     *)\n   223→(* - Therefore SAT ∉ P is mathematically true                               *)\n   224→(*                                                                           *)\n   225→(* Why Soundness Cannot Be Assumed:                                          *)\n   226→(* Proving soundness requires:                                               *)\n   227→(* 1. Every axiom of TheoryB' is TRUE about Turing machines                 *)\n   228→(* 2. Every inference rule PRESERVES truth about computation                 *)\n   229→(* 3. The encoding of computational problems into formulas is FAITHFUL      *)\n   230→(*                                                                           *)\n   231→(* Tarnlund provides none of these. As Makholm (2008) noted, even the       *)\n   232→(* axioms are not clearly enough specified to verify correctness.           *)\n   233→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   234→\n   235→(* Verification checks *)\n   236→Check tarnlund_fails_at_soundness.\n   237→Check what_would_be_needed.\n   238→Check provability_not_truth_without_soundness.\n   239→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

      {
        "tool_use_id": "toolu_01RXdqxCWwMrvw9fTjZiWZQK",
        "type": "tool_result",
        "content": "     1→/-\n     2→  FeinsteinProof.lean - Formalization of Craig Alan Feinstein's 2003-04 P≠NP proof attempt\n     3→\n     4→  This file formalizes the structure of Feinstein's argument, showing how he attempted\n     5→  to prove P ≠ NP using a restricted computational model approach.\n     6→\n     7→  NOTE: This formalization represents the CLAIMED proof structure. The errors and\n     8→  refutation are in the refutation/ directory.\n     9→\n    10→  Author: Formalization for p-vs-np repository\n    11→  Date: 2026-01-18\n    12→  Related: Issue #434, Woeginger's list entry #11\n    13→-/\n    14→\n    15→/-! # Part 1: Basic Computational Definitions -/\n    16→\n    17→/-- A problem instance (abstract representation) -/\n    18→def ProblemInstance := Nat\n    19→\n    20→/-- A solution to a problem instance -/\n    21→def Solution := Nat\n    22→\n    23→/-- An algorithm maps problem instances to solutions -/\n    24→structure Algorithm where\n    25→  solve : ProblemInstance → Solution\n    26→  runningTime : ProblemInstance → Nat\n    27→\n    28→/-- NP-complete problem (abstract) -/\n    29→structure NPCompleteProblem where\n    30→  verify : ProblemInstance → Solution → Bool\n    31→\n    32→/-! # Part 2: Restricted Computational Models -/\n    33→\n    34→/-- Abstract computational model with specific restrictions -/\n    35→structure ComputationalModel where\n    36→  allowedOperations : List String\n    37→  operationCosts : String → Nat → Nat\n    38→\n    39→/-- The unrestricted/general Turing machine model -/\n    40→def turingMachineModel : ComputationalModel := {\n    41→  allowedOperations := [\"read\", \"write\", \"move\", \"branch\"],\n    42→  operationCosts := fun _ n => n\n    43→}\n    44→\n    45→/-- Example: A restricted model that only allows certain types of operations\n    46→    This represents the kind of model Feinstein would have defined -/\n    47→def feinsteinRestrictedModel : ComputationalModel := {\n    48→  allowedOperations := [\"compare\", \"add\"],\n    49→  operationCosts := fun op n =>\n    50→    match op with\n    51→    | \"compare\" => n * n\n    52→    | \"add\" => n\n    53→    | _ => n\n    54→}\n    55→\n    56→/-- Running time of an algorithm in a specific model -/\n    57→def runningTimeInModel (alg : Algorithm) (_model : ComputationalModel) : ProblemInstance → Nat :=\n    58→  fun inst => alg.runningTime inst\n    59→\n    60→/-! # Part 3: Lower Bounds -/\n    61→\n    62→/-- An algorithm has a lower bound in a model -/\n    63→def hasLowerBound (alg : Algorithm) (model : ComputationalModel) (bound : Nat → Nat) : Prop :=\n    64→  ∀ inst, runningTimeInModel alg model inst ≥ bound inst\n    65→\n    66→/-- A problem requires at least some time in a model -/\n    67→def problemLowerBound (problem : NPCompleteProblem) (model : ComputationalModel)\n    68→    (bound : Nat → Nat) : Prop :=\n    69→  ∀ alg : Algorithm,\n    70→    (∀ inst sol, alg.solve inst = sol → problem.verify inst sol = true) →\n    71→    hasLowerBound alg model bound\n    72→\n    73→/-- Exponential lower bound -/\n    74→def exponentialBound (n : Nat) : Nat := 2^(n/2)\n    75→\n    76→/-! # Part 4: Feinstein's Claimed Proof Structure -/\n    77→\n    78→/-\n    79→  Feinstein's proof had three main steps:\n    80→\n    81→  1. CLAIM: Proved lower bound in restricted model\n    82→     - Within his restricted computational model, NP-complete problems require\n    83→       exponential time\n    84→\n    85→  2. CLAIM: Transfer principle\n    86→     - Lower bounds in the restricted model imply lower bounds in general\n    87→       Turing machine computation\n    88→\n    89→  3. CONCLUSION: P ≠ NP\n    90→     - Combining (1) and (2), NP-complete problems require exponential time\n    91→       in general, so P ≠ NP\n    92→-/\n    93→\n    94→/-- Step 1: Feinstein's claimed lower bound in restricted model\n    95→    This axiom represents what Feinstein claimed to have proven -/\n    96→axiom feinsteinRestrictedLowerBound :\n    97→  ∀ (problem : NPCompleteProblem),\n    98→    problemLowerBound problem feinsteinRestrictedModel exponentialBound\n    99→\n   100→/-- Step 2: Feinstein's transfer principle (THE ERROR)\n   101→    This axiom represents Feinstein's claimed transfer principle.\n   102→    This is where the proof fails - see refutation/ for why. -/\n   103→axiom feinsteinTransferPrinciple :\n   104→  ∀ (problem : NPCompleteProblem) (bound : Nat → Nat),\n   105→    problemLowerBound problem feinsteinRestrictedModel bound →\n   106→    problemLowerBound problem turingMachineModel bound\n   107→\n   108→/-- Step 3: Feinstein's conclusion\n   109→    Combining the restricted model lower bound with the transfer principle -/\n   110→theorem feinsteinConclusion :\n   111→  ∀ (problem : NPCompleteProblem),\n   112→    problemLowerBound problem turingMachineModel exponentialBound := by\n   113→  intro problem\n   114→  -- Apply the transfer principle to the restricted model lower bound\n   115→  apply feinsteinTransferPrinciple\n   116→  -- Use the claimed restricted model lower bound\n   117→  exact feinsteinRestrictedLowerBound problem\n   118→\n   119→/-!\n   120→# Summary\n   121→\n   122→This file formalizes the STRUCTURE of Feinstein's 2003-04 proof attempt.\n   123→The proof relies on:\n   124→\n   125→1. `feinsteinRestrictedLowerBound` - A lower bound proven within a restricted model\n   126→2. `feinsteinTransferPrinciple` - A claim that restricted model bounds transfer to general computation\n   127→3. `feinsteinConclusion` - The logical consequence of (1) and (2)\n   128→\n   129→The proof structure is valid IF the axioms are true. However, `feinsteinTransferPrinciple`\n   130→is FALSE - this is demonstrated in the refutation/ directory.\n   131→\n   132→The counterexample that invalidated Feinstein's proof showed that an algorithm\n   133→unavailable in his restricted model could solve the problem efficiently in general\n   134→computation, thereby violating the transfer principle.\n   135→-/\n   136→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. ## Section 1: Introduction - Overview of the Argument\n    21→\n    22→  From the paper (Section 1):\n    23→  \"SAT ∉ P is true, theorem 1, and provable, corollary 9, in a simply consistent\n    24→  extension B' of a first order theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom\n    25→  B characterizing a universal Turing machine.\"\n    26→-/\n    27→\n    28→/-! ## Section 2: A Theory of Computing\n    29→\n    30→  From the paper (Section 2):\n    31→  \"Before applying the axiomatic method to computing complexity, a first order\n    32→  theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal\n    33→  Turing machine, is presented.\"\n    34→-/\n    35→\n    36→/-- Binary strings as inputs -/\n    37→def Language := String → Bool\n    38→\n    39→/-- Time complexity function -/\n    40→def TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n    41→\n    42→/-- Polynomial time (Definition 8: p(a) for c·|a|^q some c q ∈ ℕ) -/\n    43→def isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    44→  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n    45→\n    46→/-- Class P: polynomial-time decidable languages -/\n    47→structure ClassP where\n    48→  language : Language\n    49→  decider : String → Nat\n    50→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    51→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    52→\n    53→/-- Class NP: polynomial-time verifiable languages -/\n    54→structure ClassNP where\n    55→  language : Language\n    56→  verifier : String → String → Bool\n    57→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    58→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    59→\n    60→/-- SAT problem (axiomatically NP-complete) -/\n    61→axiom SAT : ClassNP\n    62→\n    63→/-- P ≠ NP statement -/\n    64→def PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    65→  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n    66→\n    67→/-! ## Section 2: Formal System Definitions\n    68→\n    69→  From the paper:\n    70→  \"Syntactically, there are two predicate symbols of B written T(i, a, u), and\n    71→  U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u).\"\n    72→-/\n    73→\n    74→/-- A formula in the formal system (Definition 3) -/\n    75→structure Formula where\n    76→  symbols : List String\n    77→\n    78→/-- A formal system with axioms and inference rules -/\n    79→structure FormalSystem where\n    80→  axioms : List Formula\n    81→  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n    82→\n    83→/-- Provability in a formal system -/\n    84→def Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n    85→  True  -- Abstract provability relation\n    86→\n    87→/-! ## Theory B and Axiom B (Axiom 1)\n    88→\n    89→  From the paper (Axiom 1):\n    90→  \"B for ∀ T(i, a, u) ⊃ U(∅, ∅, a.∅, 1, i, i, u) ...\"\n    91→  The axiom has six sentences (17)-(22) characterizing a universal Turing machine.\n    92→-/\n    93→\n    94→/-- Theory B (Tarnlund's base formal system) -/\n    95→def TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    96→  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n    97→\n    98→/-- Universal Turing Machine axiom (Axiom 1, sentences 17-22) -/\n    99→noncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n   100→\n   101→/-- Theory B' (Theory B + Universal TM axiom, simply consistent extension) -/\n   102→noncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n   103→  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n   104→    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n   105→\n   106→/-! ## Section 2: Consistency Claims\n   107→\n   108→  From the paper (Corollary 2):\n   109→  \"Theory B is simply consistent in U i.e., there is no contradiction\"\n   110→-/\n   111→\n   112→/-- Consistency of a formal system -/\n   113→def IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True  -- Abstract consistency\n   114→\n   115→/-- Simple consistency (Definition from Kleene 1967) -/\n   116→def IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n   117→\n   118→/-- Tarnlund claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2) -/\n   119→axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n   120→\n   121→/-! ## Section 2: Lemma 1\n   122→\n   123→  From the paper:\n   124→  \"Lemma 1: ∃uT(i, a, u) ⊃ ⊢ B → ∃uT(i, a, u) any i ∈ M a ∈ L.\"\n   125→  If a computation terminates, it is provable in theory B.\n   126→-/\n   127→\n   128→/-- Lemma 1: True computations are provable in theory B -/\n   129→axiom lemma1_computations_provable :\n   130→  ∀ (F : Formula), Provable TheoryBPrime F\n   131→\n   132→/-! ## Section 3: Computing Time (Definitions 6-15)\n   133→\n   134→  From the paper (Definition 15):\n   135→  \"SAT ∈ P for ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, F.∅, u) in p(F) some i ∈ U any F ∈ F.\"\n   136→-/\n   137→\n   138→/-- Formula representing \"SAT ∉ P\" -/\n   139→def SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n   140→  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n   141→\n   142→/-- What it means for a formula to represent a computational fact -/\n   143→def FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n   144→  True  -- Abstract meaning relation\n   145→\n   146→/-- Tarnlund claims to prove \"SAT ∉ P\" in TheoryB' (Theorem 1) -/\n   147→axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n   148→\n   149→/-- The formula is claimed to mean P ≠ NP -/\n   150→axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n   151→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n   152→\n   153→/-! ## Section 4: Computing Time and Proof Complexity\n   154→\n   155→  From the paper (Lemma 3):\n   156→  \"⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F) ⊃ Y(i, F, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|F|^q\"\n   157→\n   158→  This is the principal lemma establishing the relationship between\n   159→  computing time and proof complexity.\n   160→-/\n   161→\n   162→/-- Y(i, F, n) formula - Definition 20\n   163→    Y(i, F, n) for (Q₀ ⊃ F) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁)\n   164→    This represents the propositional formula that relates computing time\n   165→    to a chain of implications leading to the tautology F. -/\n   166→def Y_formula (_i : Nat) (_F : Formula) (_n : Nat) : Prop := True\n   167→\n   168→/-- Lemma 3 (Principal Lemma):\n   169→    If computing time is polynomial, then Y(i, F, n) holds with polynomial n.\n   170→    This is the key claim linking computing time to proof complexity. -/\n   171→axiom lemma3_computing_time_proof_complexity :\n   172→  ∀ (i : Nat) (F : Formula) (n : Nat),\n   173→    Y_formula i F n\n   174→\n   175→/-! ## Section 4.1: Proof Complexity and Haken's Theorem\n   176→\n   177→  From the paper:\n   178→  \"Every resolution proof of PFₙ contains at least cⁿ different clauses\n   179→  for c > 1 some c ∈ ℝ any sufficiently large n ∈ ℕ\" (Haken 1985)\n   180→-/\n   181→\n   182→/-- Resolution provability (Definition 22) -/\n   183→def ResolutionProvable (_F : Formula) (_size : Nat) : Prop := True\n   184→\n   185→/-- Haken's theorem: resolution proofs of pigeonhole formulas require\n   186→    exponentially many clauses -/\n   187→axiom hakens_theorem :\n   188→  ∀ (m : Nat), ∃ (minSize : Nat),\n   189→    (∀ (k : Nat), minSize > k * m ^ k) ∧\n   190→    ∀ (size : Nat), size < minSize → ¬ ResolutionProvable\n   191→      { symbols := [\"PF\", toString m] } size\n   192→\n   193→/-! ## Main Results: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2\n   194→\n   195→  From the paper (Theorem 1 proof, steps 46-53):\n   196→  ⋆ SAT ∈ P (assumption for contradiction)\n   197→  ⋆ ⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F)  (Corollary 5)\n   198→  ⋆ Y(i, PFₘ, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|PFₘ|^q  (Lemma 3)\n   199→  ⋆ ⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ)  (from Y formula)\n   200→  ⋆ ¬(⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ))  (Haken's theorem - CONTRADICTION)\n   201→  ⋆ SAT ∉ P\n   202→-/\n   203→\n   204→/-- Soundness for computational complexity: provability implies truth -/\n   205→def IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   206→  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   207→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n   208→    Provable sys F →\n   209→    fact\n   210→\n   211→/-- IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (Theorem 1 structure)\n   212→    This captures the VALID part of Tarnlund's argument:\n   213→    given soundness + provability + meaning, the conclusion follows. -/\n   214→theorem soundness_implies_truth :\n   215→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n   216→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n   217→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n   218→  PNotEqualsNP := by\n   219→  intro soundness provable meaning\n   220→  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n   221→\n   222→/-! ## Summary of Tarnlund's Claimed Proof Structure\n   223→\n   224→  Tarnlund's approach:\n   225→  1. ✓ Defines a formal system TheoryB' (Section 2, Axiom 1)\n   226→  2. ✓ Claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n   227→  3. ✓ Establishes computing time / proof complexity relationship (Lemma 3)\n   228→  4. ✓ Uses Haken's theorem for contradiction (Theorem 1 proof)\n   229→  5. ✗ MISSING: Proof that TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n   230→\n   231→  The gap: Without soundness, provability in the system doesn't establish\n   232→  mathematical truth. The proof proves SAT ∉ P WITHIN the formal system,\n   233→  but never shows that this formal system correctly models reality.\n   234→\n   235→  NOTE: We cannot formalize the soundness proof because it does not exist\n   236→  in Tarnlund's paper. This is the critical gap identified in the refutation.\n   237→-/\n   238→\n   239→#check soundness_implies_truth\n   240→#check PNotEqualsNP\n   241→#check IsSoundForComplexity\n   242→\n   243→end TarnlundProof\n   244→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. A first-order theory B with axiom B characterizing a universal Turing machine\n  2. A relationship between computing time and proof complexity (Lemma 3)\n  3. ## Section 1: Introduction - Overview of the Argument\n\n  From the paper (Section 1):\n  \"SAT ∉ P is true, theorem 1, and provable, corollary 9, in a simply consistent\n  extension B' of a first order theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom\n  B characterizing a universal Turing machine.\"\n-/\n\n/-! ## Section 2: A Theory of Computing\n\n  From the paper (Section 2):\n  \"Before applying the axiomatic method to computing complexity, a first order\n  theory B of computing, with a single finite axiom B characterizing a universal\n  Turing machine, is presented.\"\n-/\n\n/-- Binary strings as inputs -/\ndef Language := String → Bool\n\n/-- Time complexity function -/\ndef TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n\n/-- Polynomial time (Definition 8: p(a) for c·|a|^q some c q ∈ ℕ) -/\ndef isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n\n/-- Class P: polynomial-time decidable languages -/\nstructure ClassP where\n  language : Language\n  decider : String → Nat\n  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n\n/-- Class NP: polynomial-time verifiable languages -/\nstructure ClassNP where\n  language : Language\n  verifier : String → String → Bool\n  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n\n/-- SAT problem (axiomatically NP-complete) -/\naxiom SAT : ClassNP\n\n/-- P ≠ NP statement -/\ndef PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n\n/-! ## Section 2: Formal System Definitions\n\n  From the paper:\n  \"Syntactically, there are two predicate symbols of B written T(i, a, u), and\n  U(x, s, z, q, j, i, u).\"\n-/\n\n/-- A formula in the formal system (Definition 3) -/\nstructure Formula where\n  symbols : List String\n\n/-- A formal system with axioms and inference rules -/\nstructure FormalSystem where\n  axioms : List Formula\n  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n\n/-- Provability in a formal system -/\ndef Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n  True  -- Abstract provability relation\n\n/-! ## Section 2: Consistency Claims\n\n  From the paper (Corollary 2):\n  \"Theory B is simply consistent in U i.e., there is no contradiction\"\n-/\n\n/-- Consistency of a formal system -/\ndef IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True  -- Abstract consistency\n\n/-- Simple consistency (Definition from Kleene 1967) -/\ndef IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n\n/-- Tarnlund claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2) -/\naxiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n\n/-! ## Section 3: Computing Time (Definitions 6-15)\n\n  From the paper (Definition 15):\n  \"SAT ∈ P for ⊢ B → ∃u T(i, F.∅, u) in p(F) some i ∈ U any F ∈ F.\"\n-/\n\n/-- Formula representing \"SAT ∉ P\" -/\ndef SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n\n/-- What it means for a formula to represent a computational fact -/\ndef FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n  True  -- Abstract meaning relation\n\n/-- Tarnlund claims to prove \"SAT ∉ P\" in TheoryB' (Theorem 1) -/\naxiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n\n/-- The formula is claimed to mean P ≠ NP -/\naxiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n\n/-! ## Section 4: Computing Time and Proof Complexity\n\n  From the paper (Lemma 3):\n  \"⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F) ⊃ Y(i, F, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|F|^q\"\n\n  This is the principal lemma establishing the relationship between\n  computing time and proof complexity.\n-/\n\n/-- Y(i, F, n) formula - Definition 20\n    Y(i, F, n) for (Q₀ ⊃ F) ∧ Qₙ ∧ ⋀₁≤k≤n (Qₖ ⊃ Qₖ₋₁)\n    This represents the propositional formula that relates computing time\n    to a chain of implications leading to the tautology F. -/\ndef Y_formula (_i : Nat) (_F : Formula) (_n : Nat) : Prop := True\n\n/-- Lemma 3 (Principal Lemma):\n    If computing time is polynomial, then Y(i, F, n) holds with polynomial n.\n    This is the key claim linking computing time to proof complexity. -/\naxiom lemma3_computing_time_proof_complexity :\n  ∀ (i : Nat) (F : Formula) (n : Nat),\n    Y_formula i F n\n\n/-! ## Section 4.1: Proof Complexity and Haken's Theorem\n\n  From the paper:\n  \"Every resolution proof of PFₙ contains at least cⁿ different clauses\n  for c > 1 some c ∈ ℝ any sufficiently large n ∈ ℕ\" (Haken 1985)\n-/\n\n/-- Resolution provability (Definition 22) -/\ndef ResolutionProvable (_F : Formula) (_size : Nat) : Prop := True\n\n/-- Haken's theorem: resolution proofs of pigeonhole formulas require\n    exponentially many clauses -/\naxiom hakens_theorem :\n  ∀ (m : Nat), ∃ (minSize : Nat),\n    (∀ (k : Nat), minSize > k * m ^ k) ∧\n    ∀ (size : Nat), size < minSize → ¬ ResolutionProvable\n      { symbols := [\"PF\", toString m] } size\n\n/-! ## Main Results: Theorem 1 and Theorem 2\n\n  From the paper (Theorem 1 proof, steps 46-53):\n  ⋆ SAT ∈ P (assumption for contradiction)\n  ⋆ ⊢ B → T(i, ¬F.∅, ∅.1) in p(F)  (Corollary 5)\n  ⋆ Y(i, PFₘ, n) ∧ n ≤ c·|PFₘ|^q  (Lemma 3)\n  ⋆ ⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ)  (from Y formula)\n  ⋆ ¬(⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ))  (Haken's theorem - CONTRADICTION)\n  ⋆ SAT ∉ P\n-/\n\n/-- Soundness for computational complexity: provability implies truth -/\ndef IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n    Provable sys F →\n    fact\n\n/-- IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (Theorem 1 structure)\n    This captures the VALID part of Tarnlund's argument:\n    given soundness + provability + meaning, the conclusion follows. -/\ntheorem soundness_implies_truth :\n  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n  PNotEqualsNP := by\n  intro soundness provable meaning\n  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n\n/-! ## Summary of Tarnlund's Claimed Proof Structure\n\n  Tarnlund's approach:\n  1. ✓ Defines a formal system TheoryB' (Section 2, Axiom 1)\n  2. ✓ Claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n  3. ✓ Establishes computing time / proof complexity relationship (Lemma 3)\n  4. ✓ Uses Haken's theorem for contradiction (Theorem 1 proof)\n  5. ✗ MISSING: Proof that TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n\n  The gap: Without soundness, provability in the system doesn't establish\n  mathematical truth. The proof proves SAT ∉ P WITHIN the formal system,\n  but never shows that this formal system correctly models reality.\n\n  NOTE: We cannot formalize the soundness proof because it does not exist\n  in Tarnlund's paper. This is the critical gap identified in the refutation.\n-/\n\n#check soundness_implies_truth\n#check PNotEqualsNP\n#check IsSoundForComplexity\n\nend TarnlundProof\n",

        "tool_use_id": "toolu_019Vykn7NMsLyFqBcsX1Rqv6",
        "type": "tool_result",
        "content": "     1→/-\n     2→  TarnlundRefutation.lean - Refutation of Tarnlund's 2008 P≠NP attempt\n     3→\n     4→  Original paper: \"P is not equal to NP\" (arXiv:0810.5056v1, October 2008)\n     5→\n     6→  This file demonstrates WHY Tarnlund's proof attempt fails. The key insight\n     7→  is that proving a statement within a formal system does NOT establish\n     8→  mathematical truth unless the formal system is proven SOUND for that domain.\n     9→\n    10→  Specifically, the paper's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within the formal\n    11→  system TheoryB', but never establishes that TheoryB' is sound for\n    12→  computational complexity claims. Without this soundness proof, the\n    13→  derivation is meaningless for the actual P vs NP question.\n    14→\n    15→  Critique sources:\n    16→  - Henning Makholm (2008): \"Does P equal NP? This is not an answer\"\n    17→  - The formal system's axioms are not clearly specified\n    18→  - The relationship between the formal theory and actual computation\n    19→    is not rigorously established\n    20→-/\n    21→\n    22→namespace TarnlundRefutation\n    23→\n    24→/-! ## Part 1: Definitions (same as in proof/) -/\n    25→\n    26→def Language := String → Bool\n    27→def TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n    28→\n    29→def isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n    30→  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n    31→\n    32→structure ClassP where\n    33→  language : Language\n    34→  decider : String → Nat\n    35→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    36→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    37→\n    38→structure ClassNP where\n    39→  language : Language\n    40→  verifier : String → String → Bool\n    41→  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n    42→  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n    43→\n    44→axiom SAT : ClassNP\n    45→\n    46→def PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n    47→  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n    48→\n    49→structure Formula where\n    50→  symbols : List String\n    51→\n    52→structure FormalSystem where\n    53→  axioms : List Formula\n    54→  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n    55→\n    56→def Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n    57→  True\n    58→\n    59→def TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n    60→  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n    61→\n    62→noncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n    63→\n    64→noncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n    65→  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n    66→    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n    67→\n    68→def IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True\n    69→def IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n    70→\n    71→axiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n    72→\n    73→def SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n    74→  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n    75→\n    76→def FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n    77→  True\n    78→\n    79→axiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n    80→\n    81→axiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n    82→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n    83→\n    84→def IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n    85→  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    86→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n    87→    Provable sys F →\n    88→    fact\n    89→\n    90→/-! ## Part 2: The Critical Missing Piece\n    91→\n    92→  From Henning Makholm's critique (2008):\n    93→  \"The paper is pithy to the point of sloppiness... the formal system and\n    94→  its axioms are not clearly specified... the relationship between the formal\n    95→  theory and actual Turing machine computation is not rigorously established.\"\n    96→\n    97→  Tarnlund's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within TheoryB' (steps 46-53),\n    98→  but the crucial question is: does provability within TheoryB' mean the\n    99→  statement is actually TRUE?\n   100→\n   101→  This requires a SOUNDNESS PROOF: showing that everything provable in\n   102→  TheoryB' about computational complexity is actually true about real\n   103→  Turing machines. Tarnlund never provides this proof.\n   104→-/\n   105→\n   106→/-- A soundness proof would need to demonstrate this property exists -/\n   107→def SoundnessProof (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   108→  ∃ (_proof : Unit), IsSoundForComplexity sys\n   109→\n   110→/-- THE FATAL FLAW: No soundness proof exists in the paper.\n   111→    The paper proves things WITHIN the formal system but never shows\n   112→    the formal system correctly models computational reality. -/\n   113→axiom tarnlund_no_soundness_proof : ¬ SoundnessProof TheoryBPrime\n   114→\n   115→/-! ## Part 3: Structure of the Failed Attempt -/\n   116→\n   117→structure TarnlundAttempt where\n   118→  formalSystem : FormalSystem\n   119→  formula : Formula\n   120→  provable : Provable formalSystem formula\n   121→  consistent : IsSimplyConsistent formalSystem\n   122→\n   123→/-! ## Part 4: The Refutation\n   124→\n   125→  The refutation shows that Tarnlund's attempt has all the syntactic\n   126→  components (formal system, provability, consistency) but lacks the\n   127→  semantic component (soundness) that would connect formal derivations\n   128→  to mathematical truth.\n   129→-/\n   130→\n   131→/-- Tarnlund's attempt fails because it lacks a soundness proof -/\n   132→theorem tarnlund_fails_at_soundness :\n   133→  ∃ attempt : TarnlundAttempt,\n   134→    attempt.formalSystem = TheoryBPrime ∧\n   135→    ¬ SoundnessProof attempt.formalSystem := by\n   136→  refine ⟨⟨TheoryBPrime, SATNotInP_Formula, ?_, ?_⟩, rfl, ?_⟩\n   137→  · exact tarnlund_provability_claim\n   138→  · exact tarnlund_consistency_claim\n   139→  · exact tarnlund_no_soundness_proof\n   140→\n   141→/-- The gap: What WOULD be needed for the proof to work -/\n   142→theorem what_would_be_needed :\n   143→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n   144→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n   145→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n   146→  PNotEqualsNP := by\n   147→  intro soundness provable meaning\n   148→  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n   149→\n   150→/-! ## Part 5: Why Soundness is Hard - Counterexample\n   151→\n   152→  To illustrate why soundness matters, we construct a trivially unsound\n   153→  formal system that can \"prove\" any statement (including SAT ∉ P),\n   154→  yet clearly doesn't establish mathematical truth.\n   155→\n   156→  This demonstrates that mere provability within a system, even a\n   157→  consistent one, tells us nothing about truth without soundness.\n   158→-/\n   159→\n   160→/-- Example: An unsound formal system can \"prove\" false statements -/\n   161→def UnsoundSystem : FormalSystem :=\n   162→  { axioms := [SATNotInP_Formula],\n   163→    rules := [] }\n   164→\n   165→/-- In this trivial system, \"SAT ∉ P\" is provable (it's an axiom!) -/\n   166→theorem unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p :\n   167→  Provable UnsoundSystem SATNotInP_Formula := by\n   168→  trivial\n   169→\n   170→/-- But this doesn't make it true! The system is unsound. -/\n   171→axiom unsound_system_not_sound : ¬ IsSoundForComplexity UnsoundSystem\n   172→\n   173→/-- Moral: Provability ≠ Truth without soundness -/\n   174→theorem provability_not_truth_without_soundness :\n   175→  ∃ (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   176→    Provable sys F ∧\n   177→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ∧\n   178→    ¬ IsSoundForComplexity sys := by\n   179→  refine ⟨UnsoundSystem, SATNotInP_Formula, PNotEqualsNP, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩\n   180→  · exact unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p\n   181→  · trivial  -- Abstract meaning relation\n   182→  · exact unsound_system_not_sound\n   183→\n   184→/-! ## Summary of the Refutation\n   185→\n   186→  Tarnlund's 2008 attempt failed because it conflated TWO different concepts:\n   187→\n   188→  1. PROVABILITY within a formal system (what Tarnlund established in Theorem 1)\n   189→  2. MATHEMATICAL TRUTH (what would be needed to solve P vs NP)\n   190→\n   191→  ### The Structure of the Error (referencing the paper)\n   192→\n   193→  Tarnlund showed (Theorem 1, steps 46-53):\n   194→  - TheoryB' ⊢ \"SAT ∉ P\"  (provable in the formal system)\n   195→  - TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n   196→\n   197→  But he NEEDED to additionally show:\n   198→  - TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n   199→  - Therefore provability implies truth\n   200→  - Therefore SAT ∉ P is mathematically true\n   201→\n   202→  ### Why Soundness Cannot Be Assumed\n   203→\n   204→  Proving soundness of a formal system for computational complexity requires:\n   205→  1. Every axiom of TheoryB' is TRUE as a statement about Turing machines\n   206→  2. Every inference rule PRESERVES truth about computation\n   207→  3. The encoding of computational problems into formulas is FAITHFUL\n   208→\n   209→  Tarnlund provides none of these in the paper. As Makholm (2008) noted,\n   210→  even the axioms themselves are not clearly enough specified to verify\n   211→  whether they correctly model computation.\n   212→\n   213→  ### Lessons Learned\n   214→\n   215→  1. Formal proofs require both SYNTAX (derivations) and SEMANTICS (soundness)\n   216→  2. Provability in a system ≠ mathematical truth\n   217→  3. Soundness proofs are essential but often overlooked\n   218→  4. This error pattern appears in multiple failed P vs NP attempts\n   219→-/\n   220→\n   221→#check tarnlund_fails_at_soundness\n   222→#check what_would_be_needed\n   223→#check provability_not_truth_without_soundness\n   224→\n   225→end TarnlundRefutation\n   226→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"

      "content": "/-\n  TarnlundRefutation.lean - Refutation of Tarnlund's 2008 P≠NP attempt\n\n  Original paper: \"P is not equal to NP\" (arXiv:0810.5056v1, October 2008)\n\n  This file demonstrates WHY Tarnlund's proof attempt fails. The key insight\n  is that proving a statement within a formal system does NOT establish\n  mathematical truth unless the formal system is proven SOUND for that domain.\n\n  Specifically, the paper's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within the formal\n  system TheoryB', but never establishes that TheoryB' is sound for\n  computational complexity claims. Without this soundness proof, the\n  derivation is meaningless for the actual P vs NP question.\n\n  Critique sources:\n  - Henning Makholm (2008): \"Does P equal NP? This is not an answer\"\n  - The formal system's axioms are not clearly specified\n  - The relationship between the formal theory and actual computation\n    is not rigorously established\n-/\n\nnamespace TarnlundRefutation\n\n/-! ## Part 1: Definitions (same as in proof/) -/\n\ndef Language := String → Bool\ndef TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n\ndef isPolynomial (T : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n  ∃ (c k : Nat), ∀ n : Nat, T n ≤ c * n ^ k\n\nstructure ClassP where\n  language : Language\n  decider : String → Nat\n  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n\nstructure ClassNP where\n  language : Language\n  verifier : String → String → Bool\n  timeComplexity : TimeComplexity\n  isPoly : isPolynomial timeComplexity\n\naxiom SAT : ClassNP\n\ndef PNotEqualsNP : Prop :=\n  ∀ (p : ClassP), ∃ (s : String), SAT.language s ≠ p.language s\n\nstructure Formula where\n  symbols : List String\n\nstructure FormalSystem where\n  axioms : List Formula\n  rules : List (List Formula → Formula)\n\ndef Provable (sys : FormalSystem) (_F : Formula) : Prop :=\n  True\n\ndef TheoryB : FormalSystem :=\n  { axioms := [], rules := [] }\n\nnoncomputable axiom UniversalTMAxiom : Formula\n\nnoncomputable def TheoryBPrime : FormalSystem :=\n  { axioms := UniversalTMAxiom :: TheoryB.axioms,\n    rules := TheoryB.rules }\n\ndef IsConsistent (_sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := True\ndef IsSimplyConsistent (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop := IsConsistent sys\n\naxiom tarnlund_consistency_claim : IsSimplyConsistent TheoryBPrime\n\ndef SATNotInP_Formula : Formula :=\n  { symbols := [\"SAT\", \"not\", \"in\", \"P\"] }\n\ndef FormulaMeansComputationalFact (_F : Formula) (_fact : Prop) : Prop :=\n  True\n\naxiom tarnlund_provability_claim : Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula\n\naxiom tarnlund_meaning_claim :\n  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP\n\ndef IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n  ∀ (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact →\n    Provable sys F →\n    fact\n\n/-! ## Part 2: The Critical Missing Piece\n\n  From Henning Makholm's critique (2008):\n  \"The paper is pithy to the point of sloppiness... the formal system and\n  its axioms are not clearly specified... the relationship between the formal\n  theory and actual Turing machine computation is not rigorously established.\"\n\n  Tarnlund's Theorem 1 proves \"SAT ∉ P\" within TheoryB' (steps 46-53),\n  but the crucial question is: does provability within TheoryB' mean the\n  statement is actually TRUE?\n\n  This requires a SOUNDNESS PROOF: showing that everything provable in\n  TheoryB' about computational complexity is actually true about real\n  Turing machines. Tarnlund never provides this proof.\n-/\n\n/-- A soundness proof would need to demonstrate this property exists -/\ndef SoundnessProof (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n  ∃ (_proof : Unit), IsSoundForComplexity sys\n\n/-- THE FATAL FLAW: No soundness proof exists in the paper.\n    The paper proves things WITHIN the formal system but never shows\n    the formal system correctly models computational reality. -/\naxiom tarnlund_no_soundness_proof : ¬ SoundnessProof TheoryBPrime\n\n/-! ## Part 3: Structure of the Failed Attempt -/\n\nstructure TarnlundAttempt where\n  formalSystem : FormalSystem\n  formula : Formula\n  provable : Provable formalSystem formula\n  consistent : IsSimplyConsistent formalSystem\n\n/-! ## Part 4: The Refutation\n\n  The refutation shows that Tarnlund's attempt has all the syntactic\n  components (formal system, provability, consistency) but lacks the\n  semantic component (soundness) that would connect formal derivations\n  to mathematical truth.\n-/\n\n/-- Tarnlund's attempt fails because it lacks a soundness proof -/\ntheorem tarnlund_fails_at_soundness :\n  ∃ attempt : TarnlundAttempt,\n    attempt.formalSystem = TheoryBPrime ∧\n    ¬ SoundnessProof attempt.formalSystem := by\n  refine ⟨⟨TheoryBPrime, SATNotInP_Formula, ?_, ?_⟩, rfl, ?_⟩\n  · exact tarnlund_provability_claim\n  · exact tarnlund_consistency_claim\n  · exact tarnlund_no_soundness_proof\n\n/-- The gap: What WOULD be needed for the proof to work -/\ntheorem what_would_be_needed :\n  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime →\n  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula →\n  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP →\n  PNotEqualsNP := by\n  intro soundness provable meaning\n  exact soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable\n\n/-! ## Part 5: Why Soundness is Hard - Counterexample\n\n  To illustrate why soundness matters, we construct a trivially unsound\n  formal system that can \"prove\" any statement (including SAT ∉ P),\n  yet clearly doesn't establish mathematical truth.\n\n  This demonstrates that mere provability within a system, even a\n  consistent one, tells us nothing about truth without soundness.\n-/\n\n/-- Example: An unsound formal system can \"prove\" false statements -/\ndef UnsoundSystem : FormalSystem :=\n  { axioms := [SATNotInP_Formula],\n    rules := [] }\n\n/-- In this trivial system, \"SAT ∉ P\" is provable (it's an axiom!) -/\ntheorem unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p :\n  Provable UnsoundSystem SATNotInP_Formula := by\n  trivial\n\n/-- But this doesn't make it true! The system is unsound. -/\naxiom unsound_system_not_sound : ¬ IsSoundForComplexity UnsoundSystem\n\n/-- Moral: Provability ≠ Truth without soundness -/\ntheorem provability_not_truth_without_soundness :\n  ∃ (sys : FormalSystem) (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n    Provable sys F ∧\n    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ∧\n    ¬ IsSoundForComplexity sys := by\n  refine ⟨UnsoundSystem, SATNotInP_Formula, PNotEqualsNP, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩\n  · exact unsound_proves_sat_not_in_p\n  · trivial  -- Abstract meaning relation\n  · exact unsound_system_not_sound\n\n/-! ## Summary of the Refutation\n\n  Tarnlund's 2008 attempt failed because it conflated TWO different concepts:\n\n  1. PROVABILITY within a formal system (what Tarnlund established in Theorem 1)\n  2. MATHEMATICAL TRUTH (what would be needed to solve P vs NP)\n\n  ### The Structure of the Error (referencing the paper)\n\n  Tarnlund showed (Theorem 1, steps 46-53):\n  - TheoryB' ⊢ \"SAT ∉ P\"  (provable in the formal system)\n  - TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)\n\n  But he NEEDED to additionally show:\n  - TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims\n  - Therefore provability implies truth\n  - Therefore SAT ∉ P is mathematically true\n\n  ### Why Soundness Cannot Be Assumed\n\n  Proving soundness of a formal system for computational complexity requires:\n  1. Every axiom of TheoryB' is TRUE as a statement about Turing machines\n  2. Every inference rule PRESERVES truth about computation\n  3. The encoding of computational problems into formulas is FAITHFUL\n\n  Tarnlund provides none of these in the paper. As Makholm (2008) noted,\n  even the axioms themselves are not clearly enough specified to verify\n  whether they correctly model computation.\n\n  ### Lessons Learned\n\n  1. Formal proofs require both SYNTAX (derivations) and SEMANTICS (soundness)\n  2. Provability in a system ≠ mathematical truth\n  3. Soundness proofs are essential but often overlooked\n  4. This error pattern appears in multiple failed P vs NP attempts\n-/\n\n#check tarnlund_fails_at_soundness\n#check what_would_be_needed\n#check provability_not_truth_without_soundness\n\nend TarnlundRefutation\n",
      "numLines": 226,

      "totalLines": 226
    }

: Formula) : Prop := True.\n    76→\n    77→(* ========================================================================= *)\n    78→(* Theory B and Axiom B (Axiom 1) ========================================================================= *)\n   151→\n   152→(* Definition 22: Resolution provability *)\n   153→Definition ResolutionProvable (F : Formula) (size : nat) : Prop := True.\n   154→\n   155→(* Haken's theorem *)\n   156→Axiom hakens_theorem :\n   157→  forall (m : nat), exists (minSize : nat),\n   158→    (forall (k : nat), minSize > k * m ^ k) /\\\n   159→    forall (size : nat), size < minSize ->\n   160→      ~ ResolutionProvable (mkFormula (\"PF\" :: nil) I) size.\n   161→\n   162→(* 46-53):                                           *)\n   165→(* ⋆ SAT ∈ P (assumption for contradiction)                                 *)\n   166→(* ⋆ ⊢ B → T(i,¬F.∅,∅.1) in p(F)  (Corollary 5) 169→(* ⋆ ¬(⊢_R PFₘ in p(PFₘ))  (Haken's theorem - CONTRADICTION)              *)\n   170→(* ⋆ SAT ∉ P                                                                *)\n   171→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   172→\n   173→(* Soundness for computational complexity: provability implies truth *)\n   174→Definition IsSoundForComplexity (sys : FormalSystem) : Prop :=\n   175→  forall (F : Formula) (fact : Prop),\n   176→    FormulaMeansComputationalFact F fact ->\n   177→    Provable sys F ->\n   178→    fact.\n   179→\n   180→(* IF soundness holds, THEN the proof works (valid part of argument) *)\n   181→Theorem soundness_implies_truth :\n   182→  IsSoundForComplexity TheoryBPrime ->\n   183→  Provable TheoryBPrime SATNotInP_Formula ->\n   184→  FormulaMeansComputationalFact SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP ->\n   185→  PNotEqualsNP.\n   186→Proof.\n   187→  intros soundness provable meaning.\n   188→  exact (soundness SATNotInP_Formula PNotEqualsNP meaning provable).\n   189→Qed.\n   190→\n   191→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   192→(* Summary of Tarnlund's Claimed Proof Structure                             *)\n   193→(*                                                                           *)\n   194→(* Tarnlund's approach:                                                      *)\n   195→(* 1. ✓ Defines a formal system TheoryB' (Section 2, Axiom 1)               *)\n   196→(* 2. ✓ Claims TheoryB' is simply consistent (Corollary 2)                  *)\n   197→(* 3. ✓ Establishes computing time / proof complexity relationship (Lemma 3)*)\n   198→(* 4. ✓ Uses Haken's theorem for contradiction (Theorem 1 proof)            *)\n   199→(* 5. ✗ MISSING: Proof that TheoryB' is SOUND for complexity claims         *)\n   200→(*                                                                           *)\n   201→(* The gap: Without soundness, provability in the system doesn't establish  *)\n   202→(* mathematical truth. The proof proves SAT ∉ P WITHIN the formal system,  *)\n   203→(* but never shows that this formal system correctly models reality.        *)\n   204→(*                                                                           *)\n   205→(* NOTE: We cannot formalize the soundness proof because it does not exist  *)\n   206→(* in Tarnlund's paper. This is the critical gap identified in the          *)\n   207→(* refutation.                                                               *)\n   208→(* ========================================================================= *)\n   209→\n   210→Check soundness_implies_truth.\n   211→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware.
